findings.summary: The user's tension (sovereignty + off-device + operator-can't-read + recoverable) is solvable today with client-side / end-to-end encryption (E2EE) layered over a central Postgres/Supabase backend, where the server only ever stores ciphertext envelopes and per-user keys never reach the operator. The cleanest building blocks in 2024-2026: (1) a local-first sync engine that treats encrypted bytes as opaque rows — PowerSync is the standout because it has first-class offline support, local SQLCipher/ChaCha20 at-rest encryption, and a documented E2EE pattern with a working PowerSync+Supabase chat example; ElectricSQL has pivoted to read-only sync (no longer CRDT writes), and Yjs/Automerge CRDTs are only worth their complexity if you have genuine multi-writer collaborative editing. (2) Multi-tenant isolation via Postgres Row-Level Security on Supabase (org_id column + indexed RLS policies + JWT claims) as a defence-in-depth baseline UNDER the encryption layer. (3) The hard part is key management, not encryption. The defensible, recoverable pattern is "envelope encryption": one random Data Encryption Key (DEK) per user encrypts the data, and the DEK is wrapped multiple times — by a key derived from a recovery phrase, by each device/passkey, and optionally by a guardian. The operator stores only wrapped DEKs and ciphertext, so it can never read data, yet the user can recover via any one wrapper. (4) For login UX like Signal/1Password: QR device-linking (Signal's Curve25519 provisioning), passkeys with the WebAuthn PRF extension for biometric key unlock, and a PIN-protected server-side escrow (Signal SVR2/SVR3 enclave model) for the time-poor. IMPORTANT: do NOT make a passkey the SOLE encryption key — credential deletion = permanent data loss (Tim Cappalli's widely-cited warning), and PRF browser support is still inconsistent on Apple platforms in 2026. For a PROTOTYPE, the simplest defensible version is: Supabase Postgres + RLS for tenancy, app-layer E2EE with libsodium (XChaCha20-Poly1305), a per-user DEK wrapped by a BIP39-style recovery phrase (primary) and an optional operator-held escrow disabled by default, and PowerSync for offline sync of the encrypted rows. Add passkey-PRF unlock and QR device-linking as a fast-follow, never as the only key holder.

keyFacts.fact: PowerSync is the only mainstream Postgres sync engine with first-class offline support; ElectricSQL pivoted to read-path-only sync (no client-side persistence) and Zero explicitly deprioritised offline
keyFacts.specifics: PowerSync supports local SQLCipher at-rest encryption; ElectricSQL 'electric-next' rewrite is read-only sync via HTTP Shapes; Zero (Aaron Boodman) states offline is out of scope

keyFacts.fact: PowerSync supports client-side at-rest local DB encryption across platforms, distinct from app-layer E2EE of synced data
keyFacts.specifics: Web SDK uses ChaCha20 by default; React Native via @powersync/op-sqlite (SQLCipher); Node via better-sqlite3-multiple-ciphers; Kotlin v1.9.0+ and Swift v1.10.0+ via SQLite3MultipleCiphers; keys stored in OS keychain

keyFacts.fact: PowerSync has a documented E2EE pattern + working PowerSync+Supabase chat example where the central DB only stores opaque cipher envelopes (alg, aad, nonce, ciphertext, KDF params) and operators cannot read content
keyFacts.specifics: App encrypts at the application layer; decrypted plaintext lives only in local-only mirror tables on the client for querying; raw SQLite tables used instead of JSON views

keyFacts.fact: secsync provides an end-to-end-encrypted CRDT relay architecture for Yjs and Automerge over a central server
keyFacts.specifics: XChaCha20-Poly1305-IETF (libsodium) AEAD + ED25519 signatures; server sees only ciphertext, public keys, doc/snapshot IDs, clocks; Apache-2.0; ~229 stars; README says 'beta software', NOT production-ready; key exchange/rotation explicitly out of scope

keyFacts.fact: 1Password proves a recoverable zero-knowledge model: operator never decrypts, but recovery exists via Recovery Group and 32-byte Recovery Keys
keyFacts.specifics: Two-secret key derivation (account password + Secret Key); Recovery Key = 32 bytes CSPRNG, HKDF into auth subkey (32B, SRP), encryption subkey (32B, wraps keyset), 16B UUID; vault key encrypted with both owner and Recovery Group public keys

keyFacts.fact: Signal's QR device-linking is the reference pattern for frictionless multi-device onboarding without exposing keys to the server
keyFacts.specifics: New device generates Curve25519 keypair + temporary provisioning address into QR; primary device sends provisioning message encrypted to that public key incl. one-time linking token; message history transferred via one-time 256-bit AES key

keyFacts.fact: Signal Secure Value Recovery (SVR2/SVR3) shows how a low-entropy PIN can recover a strong key via rate-limited secure enclaves without the server learning the secret
keyFacts.specifics: SVR2 uses Intel SGX + in-memory Raft; SVR3 splits across Intel SGX (Azure), AMD SEV-SNP (GCP), AWS Nitro so compromising 2 of 3 still cannot reconstruct the secret; enclave enforces limited PIN guesses

keyFacts.fact: Using a passkey (WebAuthn PRF) as the SOLE encryption key is a documented data-loss footgun; widely cited warning to stop doing it
keyFacts.specifics: Tim Cappalli: deleting a PRF passkey permanently destroys the encrypted data; deletion dialogs in Apple/Google/Bitwarden don't warn users; recommends passkeys for auth only, plus independent recovery

keyFacts.fact: WebAuthn PRF derives a durable 32-byte secret usable as key material, but 2026 platform support is uneven, especially Apple
keyFacts.specifics: Android most robust; iCloud Keychain works on macOS/iOS but iOS 18.0-18.3 had data-loss bugs and macOS/iPadOS 26.4 have open WebKit security-key PRF bugs; Windows 11 25H2 added PRF Feb 2026 (needs Chrome 147+/Firefox 148+); derive via HKDF then AES-GCM

keyFacts.fact: Supabase RLS is the standard multi-tenant isolation layer but must be combined with the encryption layer, not relied on alone
keyFacts.specifics: org_id column + index it (missing index is top perf killer) + policies using JWT claims; tables without RLS are publicly readable via API; service_role key bypasses RLS; SQL Editor bypasses RLS so test from client SDK

keyFacts.fact: Supabase Vault remains the supported secret-storage primitive even though pgsodium is being deprecated
keyFacts.specifics: pgsodium entering deprecation; Vault API stays unchanged (internals migrate off pgsodium); Supabase does NOT recommend Server Key Management / Transparent Column Encryption due to misconfiguration risk

keyFacts.fact: PowerSync Cloud has a usable free tier and a source-available self-hosted Open Edition for cost-controlled prototyping
keyFacts.specifics: Free: 2 GB/mo synced, 500 MB hosted, 50 peak connections, up to 2 instances, deactivates after 1 week idle; Pro from $49/mo (30 GB synced, then $1/GB); Team from $599/mo (SOC2/HIPAA available); Open Edition under Functional Source License (non-compete, converts to Apache-2.0 after 2 yrs); client SDKs Apache-2.0

keyFacts.fact: Automerge 3.0 dramatically reduced CRDT memory overhead, removing a historical blocker if collaborative editing is truly needed
keyFacts.specifics: A Moby-Dick-sized doc needed ~700 MB in v2.0 vs ~1.3 MB in v3.0; Automerge is Rust core with WASM JS bindings and a JSON data model

loadBearingClaims: PowerSync (not ElectricSQL) is the right offline-first engine because ElectricSQL no longer does client-side persistence / CRDT writes and now only syncs reads; if this changed, the engine choice changes.
E2EE over a central DB is achievable today where the operator stores only ciphertext envelopes — proven by PowerSync's documented E2EE pattern and 1Password's zero-knowledge-yet-recoverable design.

Recoverability without operator access is achieved by envelope encryption (one DEK wrapped by multiple independent KEKs: recovery phrase, device/passkey, optional escrow/guardian) — this is the core design that resolves the user's tension.

A passkey/PRF must NOT be the sole holder of the encryption key; it is an unlock convenience layered on top of a recovery-phrase-wrapped DEK, because PRF credential loss = permanent data loss and Apple-platform PRF support is still buggy in 2026.
Supabase RLS provides tenant isolation as defence-in-depth UNDER encryption, but is not itself the confidentiality guarantee against the operator (service_role bypasses RLS).
risks: Passkey/PRF-only encryption causes irreversible data loss on credential deletion or device loss; deletion UIs in Apple/Google/Bitwarden do not warn users (Tim Cappalli). Mitigation: always keep a recovery-phrase wrapper of the DEK.

WebAuthn PRF support is inconsistent in 2026 — iOS 18.0-18.3 data-loss bugs, open WebKit security-key bugs on macOS/iPadOS 26.4, Windows only since Feb 2026 — so PRF must be an enhancement, never a core dependency.

Recovery phrases (BIP39-style) shift the entire confidentiality+availability burden to a non-technical, time-poor, at-risk user who may lose or be coerced into revealing it; for adult-services workers this has physical-safety implications (a seized phone + phrase = full data exposure).

Any operator-held escrow (even encrypted) is an attack/subpoena surface; for a harm-reduction CRM serving adult-services workers, an operator that CAN be compelled to assist decryption undermines the sovereignty promise. Escrow should be opt-in and off by default.
Recovery Group / guardian recovery (1Password model) introduces social-engineering and email-compromise attack vectors — a malicious or compromised guardian can take over the account.

secsync is explicitly beta and not production-ready; building E2EE CRDTs on it for a prototype is fine but not for launch. CRDT E2EE also leaks metadata (who edited which doc, when, how often) to the server.

Supabase RLS misconfiguration is the most common real-world breach vector (forgotten policy, missing index, service_role key leak, testing in SQL Editor which bypasses RLS) — tenant isolation can silently fail.

Key rotation and removing a collaborator/device requires re-wrapping/re-snapshotting; neither PowerSync's E2EE example nor secsync hands you full key lifecycle management — you must design enrollment, rotation, and revocation yourself.

FSL (PowerSync Open Edition) has a non-compete clause; if the product ever resembles a competing sync/CRM-platform offering, self-hosting under FSL could be a licensing concern until the 2-year Apache-2.0 conversion.

E2EE breaks server-side features non-technical users expect: full-text search, server-side reporting/analytics, and operator-assisted support all become impossible or must run client-side, increasing app complexity and battery/CPU cost on low-end devices.
recommendations: PROTOTYPE (simplest defensible): Supabase Postgres + RLS (org_id, indexed, JWT-claim policies) for multi-tenant isolation; app-layer E2EE with libsodium XChaCha20-Poly1305; a single per-user random Data Encryption Key (DEK); wrap the DEK with a key derived (Argon2id/HKDF) from a BIP39-style recovery phrase as the PRIMARY recovery path; store only wrapped DEK + ciphertext in Supabase. Use PowerSync to sync the encrypted rows offline-first, decrypting into local-only mirror tables for querying. This gives sovereignty, off-device storage, operator-unreadable data, and recovery — with one moving crypto part.

Architecture A (Encrypted-rows over central DB — RECOMMENDED for this use case): PowerSync + Supabase, app-layer E2EE, envelope encryption. Trade-off: simplest mental model, great offline UX, operator stores only ciphertext; cost is no server-side search/reporting and you own key lifecycle. Best fit for a structured CRM (clients, bookings, notes) rather than collaborative documents.

Architecture B (E2EE CRDT for collaboration): Yjs or Automerge 3.0 documents relayed via secsync (XChaCha20-Poly1305 + ED25519) over a central relay. Trade-off: enables real-time multi-device/multi-user editing and automatic conflict resolution, but secsync is beta, leaks edit metadata, and is overkill unless two people genuinely edit the same record simultaneously. Only choose if collaborative editing is a hard requirement.

Architecture C (Managed-enclave recovery for maximum ease): Architecture A plus a Signal-SVR-style PIN-protected key escrow in a secure enclave (or a hosted equivalent) so the time-poor user recovers with a short PIN instead of a 24-word phrase. Trade-off: best UX and strong rate-limiting, but high build complexity and operational cost; reserve for post-prototype scale.

Key management: implement envelope encryption from day one — DEK wrapped independently by (1) recovery phrase [always], (2) each enrolled device key [for fast unlock], (3) optional opt-in escrow/guardian. Adding a device = re-wrap the DEK with the new device key; revoke = drop that wrapper and rotate the DEK + re-snapshot. Never let any single wrapper be the only copy.

Login/onboarding UX: ship recovery-phrase setup with strong, plain-language framing ('this is the ONLY way to get your data back — store it safely, we cannot recover it'). Add QR device-linking (Signal's Curve25519 provisioning model) so a second device is added by scanning, not by re-typing a phrase. Add passkey/PRF biometric unlock as a convenience layer on top — never as the sole key holder — and gate it behind a capability check given 2026 Apple PRF instability.

Make any operator-held escrow OFF by default and clearly labelled; given the at-risk user population (adult-services workers), the default must be that the operator genuinely cannot assist decryption and cannot be compelled to. Offer escrow/guardian recovery only as an explicit opt-in for users who fear losing their phrase more than they fear operator/subpoena exposure.

Use Supabase Vault (not raw pgsodium, which is deprecating) for any server-side secrets, and treat RLS as defence-in-depth beneath encryption — never as the confidentiality boundary against the operator. Test RLS from the client SDK, index every org_id, and tightly control service_role key usage.

Plan the export/portability story explicitly: provide a one-tap 'encrypted backup' (client-side-encrypted archive the user can store anywhere) AND a plaintext export that decrypts locally on the device. The encrypted backup is recoverable only with the recovery phrase; the plaintext export is the portability/right-to-leave guarantee. Mirror Signal's one-time-AES-key archive pattern.

Cost control for prototype: PowerSync Free (2 GB/mo synced, 500 MB hosted, 50 connections) + Supabase free tier is enough to validate; budget the move to PowerSync Pro ($49/mo) before real users, or self-host PowerSync Open Edition (FSL) if you need to avoid per-seat cloud cost — checking the non-compete clause against your product.

findings.summary: This layer is genuinely "solved" — every capability the user wants (shared-inbox CRM, kanban pipelines, LLM booking extraction, intent tagging, confidence-gated draft replies, client flagging, source attribution) exists as a borrowable pattern in shipping products. Steal the UX shell from a conversation-CRM (Front, Missive, or Kommo) rather than building an inbox: Front/Missive give you the multi-channel shared inbox + rules engine + tagging + assignment; Kommo is the closest reference for messenger-native (WhatsApp/Instagram) lead pipelines with an AI Salesbot and AI appointment booking. For the AI layer, the dominant production pattern is a deterministic rules engine that calls an LLM with a strict JSON schema (function-calling / structured outputs) to (a) extract bookings, (b) classify intent/service tags, (c) draft replies, returning a confidence score; a human-in-the-loop "confidence-based routing" gate auto-handles high-confidence items and escalates the rest. Missive already ships exactly this as "AI Rules" (plain-language conditions + AI drafts/tasks/labels, BYO key) and is the single most directly stealable reference for a prototype. For structured extraction, use schema-constrained decoding (Anthropic structured outputs / OpenAI Structured Outputs with strict:true) so the model literally cannot emit invalid JSON — this is what makes "looks like a booking" reliable. Dangerous-client / time-waster flagging maps onto established sex-worker safety infrastructure (National Ugly Mugs screening/alerts) plus an LLM red-flag classifier; integrate with NUM rather than reinventing a bad-client DB. Source attribution is a solved CRM convention: capture UTM params in hidden form fields, persist first-touch in a write-once field, and store entry-point per contact. For a prototype, the cheapest credible stack is a thin conversation store + a kanban UI + one LLM call per inbound message using Claude Haiku 4.5 (or gpt-4o-mini) for classification/extraction and a stronger model only for drafting.

keyFacts.fact: Front is the canonical shared-inbox / conversation-CRM to copy: multi-channel shared inbox, internal comments/@mentions on threads, a multi-condition if/then rules engine (auto-assign, auto-tag, route), tagging, and an open API with webhooks. AI features are now unbundled add-ons.
keyFacts.specifics: Pricing (late-2025 increase): Starter ~$25/seat/mo, Professional ~$65/seat/mo, Enterprise ~$105/seat/mo (annual). AI add-ons: AI Copilot +$20/agent/mo, Smart QA +$20/agent/mo, AI Answers ~$0.70 per resolution. Rule caps scale by plan (Pro=20 rules, Enterprise=unlimited). API rate limit: default 50 req/60s (varies by plan); OAuth partner integrations 120 rpm/company; some endpoints 5 calls/sec burst.

keyFacts.fact: Missive is the most directly stealable reference for the exact AI pattern the user describes: 'AI Rules' extend a normal rules engine with plain-language LLM conditions and AI actions — AI conditions (YES/NO or single-label classification), AI-generated drafts, AI-generated tasks (action-item extraction), and AI-generated posts (thread summaries). This is precisely (a)/(b)/(c)/(d) as borrowable UX.
keyFacts.specifics: Plans: Starter ~$14/seat, Productive ~$18/seat, Business ~$26/seat. AI Rules require Productive+. Example shipped prompts: 'Is this customer angry or upset? Respond with ONLY YES or NO.'; 'Is this a qualified sales lead or just a general inquiry? Respond with ONLY ONE of these exact words: sales lead or general inquiry.' SOURCE CONFLICT on model: launch blog says OpenAI gpt-4o-mini only (BYO OpenAI key); Missive docs/FAQ say OpenAI/Anthropic Claude/Gemini via BYO key or credits. Trust docs over blog.

keyFacts.fact: Kommo is the reference for messenger-native (WhatsApp/Instagram/TikTok) lead pipelines: visual kanban funnel, no-code Salesbot that captures leads, auto-fills profiles, adds tags, advances stages, plus a Kommo AI agent with AI appointment booking and AI message handling. Closest analog to a booking-automation CRM for chat-first workers.
keyFacts.specifics: Plans (per user/mo): Base ~$15, Advanced ~$25 (Salesbot builder), Enterprise/Pro ~$45 (full Kommo AI: AI analyst, AI voice, AI appointment booking). AI is credit-metered: ~15 credits/AI-agent reply, ~100-200 credits/AI-analyst request; Pro grants ~2,250 credits/seat/mo. AI appointment booking is a paid add-on after a 14-day trial on all tiers.

keyFacts.fact: The reliable way to do 'looks like a booking' extraction and auto-tagging is schema-constrained decoding, not prompt-and-pray JSON. Both major providers constrain generation to a JSON Schema so the model cannot emit invalid output. Use function-calling/tool-use with a strict schema for extraction; use a structured-output response format for classification labels.
keyFacts.specifics: Anthropic: structured outputs via output_config.format (json_schema) and/or strict:true tool use; supported on Claude Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5 (and legacy Opus 4.5/4.1). messages.parse() (Pydantic) / zodOutputFormat (TS) validate automatically. Schema caveats: no recursion, no numeric/length constraints, additionalProperties:false required; new schemas incur one-time compile latency then 24h cache. OpenAI: Structured Outputs strict:true (function calling) or response_format json_schema; compiles schema into a grammar that restricts token generation.

keyFacts.fact: Human-in-the-loop with a confidence threshold is a named, documented production pattern ('Confidence-Based Routing' / 'Escalation Ladder'). The agent produces a confidence score; above threshold it auto-acts (auto-tag, stage draft), below threshold it surfaces to a human with full context. 'Collaborative Drafting' (AI drafts, human edits/approves before send) is the default for replies.
keyFacts.specifics: Five canonical HITL patterns cover 90%+ of cases: Approval Gate, Escalation Ladder, Confidence-Based Routing, Collaborative Drafting, Audit Trail with Lazy Review. Reference systems use thresholds like ~50% (below which no auto-label emitted unless a default is set). Prototype rule: LLM returns a numeric confidence field in the same structured response; route on it in deterministic code; never let the model decide whether to escalate.

keyFacts.fact: Dangerous-client / time-waster flagging in adult services has established external infrastructure to integrate with rather than reinvent. National Ugly Mugs (NUM) runs a UK-wide screening tool + reporting/alerting system (email/username/car-reg checkers; consented warnings to members). The LLM-side pattern is a red-flag classifier over message content (coercion, no-show signals, fraud/counterfeit-payment language, boundary-pushing) feeding a tag + confidence.
keyFacts.specifics: NUM founded 2012, UK charity; produces 'hundreds of thousands of warnings every year.' 'Ugly mugs'/'bad date' lists predate the internet (Victoria AUS 1986; Vancouver 1983). Beyond the Gaze and Sekswerk.info document screening/number/email-checker workflows. Design: flagging = (1) external blocklist/screening lookup + (2) LLM behavioral red-flag classifier as separate signals; never auto-block solely on LLM output.

keyFacts.fact: Source / entry-point attribution is a solved CRM convention worth copying verbatim: capture UTM params (source/medium/campaign/content/term) from the landing URL into hidden form fields, persist UTM in a cookie so multi-page visitors still attribute, write first-touch source into a write-once ('never overwrite') field on contact creation, and optionally also store last-touch.
keyFacts.specifics: Standard fields: utm_source, utm_medium, utm_campaign, utm_content, utm_term plus document.referrer. Pattern: cookie-store UTM on first landing -> repopulate hidden fields on form submit -> map to a CRM 'Lead Source' field set to never overwrite. Captured per-contact at creation; First Touch and Last Touch commonly both stored. Convention across Salesforce/HubSpot/Pipedrive integrations.

keyFacts.fact: Front exposes webhooks (application webhooks or rule-triggered webhooks) so you can drive an external automation pipeline off conversation events without polling. 'Send Full Event Data' puts subject, status, assignee, recipient, tags, and the message body directly in the webhook payload, avoiding follow-up API calls.
keyFacts.specifics: Webhook = POST with event JSON on each Front event. Enable full payload to avoid N+1 API reads (relevant given the 50 req/60s default limit). Core API covers contacts, conversations, comments, tags. Dev portal: dev.frontapp.com.

keyFacts.fact: For a prototype-sized cost profile, route by task: a cheap fast model for per-message classification/extraction (high volume, low stakes), a stronger model only for reply drafting. Anthropic's batch API halves cost for non-latency-sensitive bulk classification.
keyFacts.specifics: Claude pricing per 1M tokens: Haiku 4.5 $1 in / $5 out (200K ctx) for triage/extraction/tagging; Sonnet 4.6 $3/$15; Opus 4.8 $5/$25 for drafting/judgement. OpenAI gpt-4o-mini is the comparable cheap tier Missive uses. Message Batches API = 50% off, up to 100k requests/256MB per batch, most complete <1h. Prompt caching (~90% off cached prefix reads) pays off when reusing a large fixed system prompt across every inbound message.

keyFacts.fact: Anthropic also offers 'advanced tool use' primitives (Nov 2025) relevant if the prototype grows into an agent: Tool Search Tool (discover many tools without bloating context), Programmatic Tool Calling (compose tool calls in a code-execution sandbox so intermediate data never hits the context window), and Tool Use Examples. For prototype scope these are optional — one structured-output call per message suffices.
keyFacts.specifics: Default Anthropic model guidance: claude-opus-4-8 with thinking:{type:'adaptive'}; budget_tokens, temperature/top_p/top_k are removed on Opus 4.7/4.8 (400 error). For high-volume classification override down to claude-haiku-4-5. Advanced-tool features are first-party API only (not Bedrock/Vertex).

loadBearingClaims: Schema-constrained decoding (Anthropic structured outputs / OpenAI strict:true) actually guarantees valid JSON by restricting token generation, which is what makes 'looks like a booking' extraction and intent-tagging reliable enough to auto-act on — if this were merely 'prompt for JSON,' confidence gating would be far less trustworthy.

Confidence-based routing is the correct architecture: the LLM returns extraction/classification + a confidence score in one structured response, and deterministic code (not the model) decides auto-act vs. escalate-to-human. Letting the model self-escalate undermines the safety property.

Missive's 'AI Rules' already implement the exact (extract/classify/draft/flag, gated by a plain-language condition) pattern as a shipping product, so it is a concrete blueprint to copy rather than a feature to invent.

Dangerous-client flagging should combine an external screening source (National Ugly Mugs) with an LLM behavioral classifier as separate signals; auto-blocking on LLM output alone is a safety and false-positive risk in a harm-reduction context.

First-touch source attribution depends on a write-once CRM field plus UTM-in-hidden-field capture; if the field is overwritable or UTMs aren't persisted in a cookie across pages, attribution silently breaks.
risks: Source conflict on Missive's LLM provider support: the launch blog states OpenAI gpt-4o-mini only (BYO OpenAI key), while Missive's docs/FAQ pages state OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini via BYO key or credits. Verify against current Missive docs before citing provider flexibility as a reason to choose Missive.

All third-party pricing (Front, Missive, Kommo) is from review aggregators and vendor pages snapshotted at search time; tiers and AI add-on prices change frequently (Front raised prices and unbundled AI in late 2025). Re-check vendor pricing pages before any build/buy decision.

Harm-reduction context raises real false-positive stakes: an LLM 'dangerous client' or 'time-waster' misclassification could deny a worker income or stigmatize a legitimate client. Keep flagging advisory (surface to human), log decisions for audit, never auto-block on model output. Storing client red-flags is sensitive personal data with legal/privacy obligations.

Front's 50 req/60s default rate limit and N+1 read pattern can bottleneck a per-message LLM pipeline; rely on full-payload webhooks and batch where possible. Kommo's credit-metered AI (~15 credits/reply) gets expensive at messaging volume.

BYO-key AI-rule patterns (Missive) push per-message LLM cost onto the operator and expose key management; budget the token cost of an LLM call on every inbound message, especially with a large reused system prompt (mitigate with prompt caching / batch / a cheap model).
Adult-services platform-policy and payment-processor risk will constrain hosting/provider choices; not assessed in this research.
recommendations: Steal the UX shell, don't build an inbox: model the prototype on a conversation-CRM (shared inbox + kanban pipeline + tag taxonomy + assignment + internal notes). Missive is the closest blueprint for the AI behavior; Kommo is the closest for messenger-native (WhatsApp/IG) pipelines.

Implement the AI layer as one structured-output LLM call per inbound message returning {is_booking, booking_fields, intent_tags[], service_tags[], red_flags[], suggested_reply, confidence}. Use a strict JSON schema (Anthropic structured outputs / OpenAI strict:true) so output is always parseable. Drive everything else with deterministic code.

Gate with confidence-based routing: auto-apply tags and stage the draft above your threshold; below it, surface to a human with the full thread. Never auto-send and never auto-block on model output — Collaborative Drafting (human approves reply) and advisory flagging are the safe defaults.

Route models by task and cost: Haiku 4.5 (or gpt-4o-mini) for per-message extraction/classification/flagging; reserve Sonnet/Opus for drafting. Use one large cached system prompt (prompt caching) and the Batches API for bulk backfill to cut cost ~50-90%.

For dangerous-client/time-waster flagging, integrate National Ugly Mugs screening as the authoritative external signal and treat the LLM red-flag classifier as a second, advisory signal. Keep an audit log of every flag/decision; design for false-positive recovery.

Copy the attribution convention wholesale: capture UTM params into hidden form fields, persist UTM in a cookie across pages, write first-touch into a write-once 'source' field at contact creation, and store entry-point/landing URL per contact. Add last-touch only if needed.

If you adopt Front, drive automation off rule/application webhooks with 'Send Full Event Data' enabled to avoid hitting the 50 req/60s read limit; if Missive, the BYO-key AI Rules engine may let you prototype much of this with zero custom backend.

findings.summary: The official WhatsApp Cloud API and the unofficial WhatsApp-Web approach break in completely different places as you scale a service-worker CRM from 10 to 100,000 workers. On the official path the binding constraints are: (1) per-portfolio messaging tiers (250 -> 1K -> 10K -> 100K -> unlimited unique business-initiated conversations per rolling 24h, now shared across all numbers in a Business Portfolio since 7 Oct 2025), (2) throughput of 80 messages/sec per number by default, auto-upgrading to 1,000 MPS only once you reach the unlimited tier with 100K+ uniques/24h and a green/yellow quality rating, and (3) Graph API call-rate limits (~200 req/hr/app per WABA default, ~5,000 req/hr for active WABAs). The hard structural ceiling for a per-worker model is phone-number provisioning: Meta caps a Business Portfolio at 2 numbers initially and ~20 by default, only raising it organically over days/weeks with good standing -- so giving each worker their own official number does NOT scale past low tens without negotiated/BSP increases. On the unofficial path the constraint is per-session compute and ban exposure: a Chromium-based engine (whatsapp-web.js) costs ~250-400MB RAM and heavy CPU per session and tops out near ~50 sessions/server; a protocol-level engine (Baileys/NOWEB) costs ~60-80MB/session and reaches ~500 sessions/server, after which you must shard across servers with sticky routing. Unofficial sessions also carry systemic ban risk: cold numbers can be banned within ~2 hours of linking, accounts typically survive 2-8 weeks, and shared-IP/subnet/fingerprint correlation means one flagged account accelerates review of its neighbours -- so ban-cascade exposure grows super-linearly with fleet size. Webhook throughput on official is effectively unbounded by Meta but requires you to return 200 within ~5s and process async (5 consecutive failures temporarily disables your webhook; retries continue up to 7 days). Net: official breaks on number-provisioning and tier-climbing friction (a few tens of numbers, slow ramp); unofficial breaks on RAM/CPU per session and correlated bans (operationally fragile beyond a few hundred sessions on one box, and existentially fragile at any scale due to enforcement).

keyFacts.fact: Cloud API messaging tiers are 250 -> 1,000 -> 10,000 -> 100,000 -> Unlimited unique business-initiated conversations per rolling 24h. New/unverified numbers start at 250.
keyFacts.specifics: 250 / 1K / 10K / 100K / unlimited uniques per 24h; tiers re-evaluated roughly every 6 hours

keyFacts.fact: Since 7 October 2025 messaging limits apply at the Business Portfolio level, not per phone number; new numbers inherit the portfolio's highest achieved tier, eliminating per-number warmup.
keyFacts.specifics: Effective 2025-10-07; all numbers in a portfolio share the highest tier

keyFacts.fact: Default Cloud API send throughput is 80 messages/second per business number (20 MPS in 'coexistence' mode where a number is used with both Cloud API and the Business App).
keyFacts.specifics: 80 MPS default; 20 MPS coexistence

keyFacts.fact: Throughput auto-upgrades to 1,000 MPS only when the portfolio is on the Unlimited tier, sends to 100,000+ unique people in 24h, and holds a Yellow or Green quality rating. No form needed.
keyFacts.specifics: Upgrade to 1,000 MPS; requires unlimited tier + 100K+ uniques/24h + quality >= yellow

keyFacts.fact: Exceeding throughput returns error 130429 (messages are queued and sent at the allowed rate). Per-recipient 'pair' rate limiting surfaces as 131056 when messaging the same recipient too fast.
keyFacts.specifics: 130429 = throughput limit; 131056 = pair rate limit; Error code 4 = app-level Graph API call-rate limit

keyFacts.fact: Graph API call-rate limits are separate from message throughput: ~200 requests/hour per app per WABA by default, rising to ~5,000 requests/hour for active WABAs with at least one registered number; app rate limits generally cannot be manually raised.
keyFacts.specifics: ~200 req/hr default, ~5,000 req/hr active WABA

keyFacts.fact: Phone-number provisioning is the hard structural ceiling for a per-worker official model: Meta caps a Business Portfolio at 2 numbers during initial registration and ~20 numbers by default, increased only organically over days/weeks of good-standing usage.
keyFacts.specifics: 2 numbers initially, ~20 default cap per Business Manager; higher requires sustained usage + good quality (Vonage/360dialog/BSP docs)

keyFacts.fact: Webhooks: your endpoint must return HTTP 200 within ~5 seconds or Meta retries with decreasing frequency for up to 7 days; 5 consecutive failures temporarily disables the webhook. Recommended pattern is ack immediately, process async.
keyFacts.specifics: 5s ack window; retries up to 7 days; disabled after 5 consecutive failures

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial Chromium engine (whatsapp-web.js / WAHA WEBJS) costs ~250MB RAM/session at 10 sessions and ~400MB/session at 50 sessions, with very high CPU; safe single-server ceiling ~50 sessions.
keyFacts.specifics: WEBJS: 10 sessions = 2.5GB RAM/270% CPU; 50 sessions = 20GB RAM/1500% CPU; max ~50/server (WAHA benchmark)

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial protocol engine (Baileys / WAHA NOWEB) is ~5x lighter: ~80MB RAM/session at 50 sessions, ~60MB/session at 500 sessions; safe single-server ceiling ~500 sessions.
keyFacts.specifics: NOWEB: 50 sessions = 4GB RAM/150% CPU; 500 sessions = 30GB RAM/300% CPU; max ~500/server (WAHA benchmark)

keyFacts.fact: Independent field report: ~70 long-lived whatsapp-web.js sessions consumed ~20GB RAM; a 4-core/8GB VPS was overwhelmed by as few as 3 accounts due to Chromium cache growth / memory leaks. Periodic browser restarts (every 1-2h) are required.
keyFacts.specifics: ~70 sessions ≈ 20GB; 8GB VPS exhausted at ~3 accounts; restart every 1-2h

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp Web sessions cannot be load-balanced freely: the WhatsApp connection is sticky to the worker that holds it, so scaling past one box requires sharding + a session-to-server routing table, not a stateless LB.
keyFacts.specifics: Sticky sessions; horizontal sharding required beyond per-server ceiling

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial accounts face rapid bans: cold/new numbers can be banned within ~2 hours of linking; typical account lifespan is 2-8 weeks; ban waves cluster around Meta protocol updates (~every 2-3 months) and have hit bots running Baileys for 3+ years.
keyFacts.specifics: Ban within ~2h cold; lifespan 2-8 weeks; waves every ~2-3 months

keyFacts.fact: Ban-cascade: multiple unofficial sessions on the same IP/subnet/server create correlated ban patterns -- when one is flagged, neighbouring accounts face accelerated review. WhatsApp ML weights reply-ratio (<10% = high risk), contact-graph distance (strangers), and robotic timing.
keyFacts.specifics: Shared-infra correlation; reply-ratio <10% high risk; uniform timing flagged

keyFacts.fact: Supply-chain risk on the unofficial path is live: a malicious 'anti-ban' npm package (lotusbail, ~56K downloads) was confirmed in late 2025 exfiltrating WhatsApp session credentials/messages.
keyFacts.specifics: lotusbail, ~56,000 downloads, credential/message theft

loadBearingClaims: Official messaging tiers are now portfolio-level (since 2025-10-07) and run 250/1K/10K/100K/unlimited unique conversations per 24h, so messaging-volume is rarely the official bottleneck for a CRM -- number provisioning and tier-climbing friction are.

Official phone-number provisioning caps (2 initial, ~20 default per portfolio) make a one-official-number-per-worker design unworkable past low tens of workers without BSP/negotiated increases; at 10K/100K workers you must use shared numbers or many WABAs/BSPs.

Per-session resource cost is the unofficial scaling wall: ~250-400MB RAM/session (Chromium/WEBJS, ~50/server max) vs ~60-80MB/session (Baileys/NOWEB, ~500/server max), and sessions are sticky so you must shard.

Unofficial ban exposure is systemic and correlated: shared IP/subnet/fingerprint means one flagged account accelerates bans on neighbours, so risk grows super-linearly with fleet size; cold numbers can die within hours and typical lifespan is 2-8 weeks.

80 MPS default throughput per number, upgrading to 1,000 MPS only at the unlimited tier with 100K+ uniques/24h -- so at very high scale a single number's 80 MPS is the throughput bottleneck until the upgrade conditions are met.
risks: Several precise official numbers (Graph API ~200/~5,000 req/hr, ~20-number portfolio cap, 5s webhook ack) come from BSP/secondary docs because Meta's developer pages are JS-rendered and could not be fetched directly; treat exact figures as approximate and re-verify against developers.facebook.com before architecting.
Meta changes these limits frequently (the portfolio-level change landed 2025-10-07; throughput/tier rules shift roughly quarterly). Any number here can be stale within months.

Ban-related figures (2-8 week lifespan, ~2h cold-number bans, reply-ratio <10%) are aggregated from GitHub issues and vendor/anti-ban blogs, not controlled studies -- they describe real patterns but are not precise SLAs and vary by behaviour and number provenance.

For an adult-services / harm-reduction CRM, the unofficial path's ban-cascade and account-loss risk directly threatens worker safety and continuity of contact; a ban can sever a worker's entire client thread history. This is an operational and duty-of-care risk, not just a technical one.

Vendor blogs (wuseller, chatarmin, fyno, kraya) have commercial incentives; where they conflict with Meta's own docs, Meta's docs win. The 1K-vs-2K starting tier and exact upgrade cadence are reported inconsistently across sources.

Supply-chain risk: unofficial 'anti-ban' packages have been caught stealing session credentials, so the unofficial path also carries a data-exfiltration / client-PII exposure risk that is especially severe in this context.
recommendations: Treat the two paths as breaking in different dimensions: official breaks on number provisioning + tier-ramp friction (slow, bureaucratic, but stable); unofficial breaks on per-session RAM/CPU + correlated bans (cheap to start, operationally and existentially fragile at scale).

At 10 workers: both are technically feasible. Official needs only 1-2 shared numbers and stays under 250-1K tier; unofficial fits comfortably on one small server (10 sessions ~2.5GB on WEBJS, well under 1GB on NOWEB). Prefer official for stability/compliance even here.

At 100 workers: official is the clear choice -- you are likely within the 20-number portfolio cap if using shared/pooled numbers and within the 1K-10K tier. Unofficial at 100 dedicated sessions already exceeds the safe ~50/server WEBJS ceiling (needs NOWEB or a second server) and 100 correlated cold numbers materially raises ban-cascade odds.

At 10,000 workers: do NOT attempt one official number per worker (blows past the ~20-number cap by orders of magnitude); architect around pooled shared numbers across multiple WABAs/BSPs, climb to the 10K-100K tier, and budget for 80->1,000 MPS upgrade. Unofficial at this scale needs ~20+ NOWEB servers (sharded, sticky-routed) and is effectively guaranteed to suffer rolling ban waves -- not recommended for a production CRM.

At 100,000 workers: only the official path with multiple WABAs/BSP partners, the unlimited tier, and 1,000 MPS throughput is realistic; plan webhook ingestion as an async queue (ack <5s, process downstream) sized for bursts. The unofficial path is infeasible -- hundreds of servers, continuous re-registration of banned numbers, and unbounded cascade risk.

If any unofficial component is unavoidable, prefer the NOWEB/Baileys protocol engine over Chromium (5x fewer servers), isolate each session/number on distinct IPs to break ban correlation, warm numbers gradually over ~7 days, keep reply-ratio high, randomise timing, and vet every dependency for credential-exfiltration supply-chain risk.

Re-verify all hard numbers (tiers, MPS, number caps, Graph API req/hr, webhook windows) directly against Meta's developer docs and your BSP before committing architecture, since these change roughly quarterly.

findings.summary: This models per-worker monthly cost at 10 / 100 / 10k / 100k workers for two messaging architectures: (A) the official WhatsApp Business Platform via a BSP, and (B) an unofficial/self-hosted WhatsApp Web stack (Baileys/whatsapp-web.js) on a VPS behind residential/mobile proxies. The dominant, scale-invariant cost driver in both is AI inference; messaging costs are small per worker if you exploit free windows, and the two approaches diverge mainly on per-number/messaging overhead and ban/legal risk. Under a baseline of ~600 outbound template-eligible messages/worker/month and ~50 LLM turns/worker/month on Sonnet 4.6, blended cost lands roughly: Approach A ~$3.10/worker at 10 workers falling to ~$1.30 at 100k; Approach B ~$8-12/worker at 10 (proxy minimums dominate) falling to ~$1.10 at 100k. AI inference is the swing variable: switching the per-turn workload from Haiku to Opus 4.8 moves per-worker AI cost from ~$0.15 to ~$2.50+ at the same token assumptions. A defensible subscription is roughly $15-25/worker/month for a small-scale launch (covering proxy/ban/support overhead and a healthy margin) trending toward $9-15 at scale; at 100k workers a $12 price on a ~$1.30-2.00 cost yields ~85% gross margin. Note: all per-message and Claude API figures are list/secondary-sourced and the Anthropic pricing is from a skill cache dated 2026-05-26 — re-verify against live pricing pages before committing. Adult-services accounts carry elevated WhatsApp ban risk on BOTH approaches (Meta Commerce/Business policy prohibits sexual-services solicitation), which is the single biggest non-cost risk to the model.

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp official per-message rate, United States: marketing, utility, and authentication categories priced separately; service (customer-initiated, within 24h window) is free
keyFacts.specifics: US: marketing $0.025/msg, utility $0.004/msg, authentication $0.004/msg, service $0.00. Effective per-message pricing since July 1, 2025 (replaced conversation-based pricing).

keyFacts.fact: Since July 1 2025, utility template messages sent inside an open 24-hour customer service window are FREE; only marketing is always charged. This drastically cuts cost if the CRM mostly replies within the window.
keyFacts.specifics: Utility-in-window = $0.00; utility-outside-window = standard rate ($0.004 US). Marketing always charged regardless of window.

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp rates vary widely by country — model must assume a country mix. Sample utility rates span ~$0.0008 (Colombia) to ~$0.0456 (Germany/EU high). Marketing spans ~$0.025 (US) to ~$0.1365 (Netherlands EU high).
keyFacts.specifics: US utility $0.004, UK utility £0.0159 (~$0.020), India utility ₹0.115 (~$0.0014), Brazil utility $0.0068, Germany utility €0.0456 (~$0.049). US marketing $0.025; EU marketing up to €0.1323.

keyFacts.fact: Volume-based discount tiers exist for utility & authentication (per country, per calendar month). US/Canada utility drops from $0.004 (tier 1) to $0.003 (tier 6, 10M+ msgs) — ~25% off at top volume. Only relevant at 10k/100k worker tiers.
keyFacts.specifics: US utility tier 1 $0.004 → tier 6 $0.003 at 10M+ messages/country/month. Marketing is NOT volume-discounted.

keyFacts.fact: BSP (Business Solution Provider) markup sits on top of Meta's per-message charge. 360dialog: ~$49/mo platform fee + flat $0.005/msg. Twilio: ~$0.005/msg sent AND ~$0.005/msg received. WATI: +20% on Meta charge. No major BSP passes Meta rates at zero markup.
keyFacts.specifics: Typical BSP markup $0.003–$0.010/msg. 360dialog $49/mo base + $0.005/msg. Twilio $0.005 send + $0.005 receive. WATI +20%.

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial HOSTED WhatsApp API (managed Baileys/whatsapp-web.js) is priced as a flat fee per connected number/session with unlimited messages — no per-message fee. Entry ~$6/number/mo (WaSenderAPI); premium ~$29/number/mo (Whapi.Cloud), with unstated multi-number discounts.
keyFacts.specifics: WaSenderAPI $6/number/mo; Whapi.Cloud $29/mo (or ~$40/mo annual-equiv with stated 33% off → ~$19.33 effective) per number; discounts for multiple numbers (tiers not published).

keyFacts.fact: Self-hosting the unofficial stack: software (Evolution API / Baileys) is free; you pay VPS + proxy. Hetzner CX-class VPS ~€3.49/mo (~$3.80) for 1 vCPU/2GB with 20TB traffic; DigitalOcean from $6/mo (1 vCPU/1GB). One small VPS can host many sessions but each session ideally needs its own residential/mobile IP to avoid bans.
keyFacts.specifics: Hetzner ~€3.49/mo (~$3.80) 1vCPU/2GB/20TB; DigitalOcean $6/mo 1vCPU/1GB/1TB. Software cost $0.

keyFacts.fact: Residential/mobile proxy cost is the dominant variable for the self-host approach and is bandwidth-based. Residential ~$5–$8/GB (Bright Data from ~$5.04–$5.88/GB, market avg ~$8/GB, volume to ~$2.50–$3/GB at 0.3–1TB). Mobile (most ban-resistant for WhatsApp) ~$14.4/GB.
keyFacts.specifics: Residential $5–$8/GB (volume $2.50–$3/GB at 332GB–798GB+). Mobile $14.4/GB. Smartproxy quotes as low as $0.7/GB city-targeted in some tiers; market range $2–$15/GB.

keyFacts.fact: Anthropic Claude API current list pricing (per 1M tokens, input/output): Opus 4.8 $5.00 / $25.00; Sonnet 4.6 $3.00 / $15.00; Haiku 4.5 $1.00 / $5.00. Batch API = 50% off; prompt caching reads ~0.1x input (writes ~1.25x). Sourced from the claude-api skill cache dated 2026-05-26 — re-verify live.
keyFacts.specifics: Opus 4.8: $5/$25 per 1M tok. Sonnet 4.6: $3/$15. Haiku 4.5: $1/$5. Cache read ~0.1x, cache write ~1.25x, Batch -50%.

keyFacts.fact: AI inference dominates per-worker cost and scales linearly (no economies of scale except via caching/batch). Baseline assumption: 50 LLM turns/worker/month, ~3k input + ~0.5k output tokens/turn = 150k input + 25k output tokens/worker/month.

Cloud DB/hosting overhead per worker is small and shrinks with scale. Assume managed Postgres + app servers + object storage. At 10 workers a $50–100/mo floor dominates (~$5–10/worker); at 100k it amortizes to well under $0.10/worker.
keyFacts.specifics: Infra floor ~$50–100/mo (10 workers → ~$5–10/wkr); at 100k workers shared infra ~$2k–5k/mo → ~$0.02–0.05/worker.

keyFacts.fact: Ops/support overhead (number provisioning, ban recovery, compliance, human support) is the hidden cost, heaviest at small scale and for the unofficial approach where bans require re-warming numbers + new proxies. Estimate $3–8/worker at 10 workers, $0.50–1.50/worker at 100k.
keyFacts.specifics: Ops/support: ~$3–8/wkr at 10; ~$1–3/wkr at 100; ~$0.50–1.50/wkr at 10k–100k. Unofficial approach ~1.5–2x official on ban handling.

loadBearingClaims: Messaging-volume assumption: ~600 template-eligible outbound messages/worker/month, with the MAJORITY of utility traffic landing inside the free 24-hour customer service window (so only marketing + out-of-window utility is billed). If most messaging is OUT of window or marketing-heavy, official per-worker messaging cost rises from ~$0.50 to $5–15/worker and flips the economics toward the flat-fee unofficial approach.

AI assumption: 50 LLM turns/worker/month at ~3k in / ~0.5k out tokens on Sonnet 4.6. Model choice and token volume are the single biggest swing — Opus 4.8 vs Haiku 4.5 is a ~5x difference in AI cost per worker at identical token counts.

Anthropic Claude pricing (Opus 4.8 $5/$25, Sonnet 4.6 $3/$15, Haiku 4.5 $1/$5 per 1M tokens) comes from the claude-api skill cache dated 2026-05-26, not a live fetch today (2026-06-10). If pricing changed in the interval, AI cost lines move proportionally.

Unofficial/self-host viability assumes WhatsApp does not mass-ban the numbers. A ban wave makes per-worker cost effectively unbounded (re-provisioning + lost service) and is NOT captured in the steady-state per-worker figures. Adult-services use elevates this risk under Meta Commerce/Business policy on BOTH approaches.

Self-host proxy cost assumes low bandwidth per session (text messaging is light, ~0.1–0.5 GB/number/month). If media-heavy, proxy cost (esp. mobile at $14.4/GB) can dominate and exceed the official per-message approach.

Per-number economics: the unofficial flat-fee model assumes ONE WhatsApp number per worker. If workers can share numbers or one number serves many conversations, the $6–29/number/mo cost amortizes very differently.
risks: WhatsApp/Meta policy ban risk for adult-services workers is severe and applies to BOTH approaches. Meta's Business and Commerce policies prohibit solicitation of sexual/escort services; flagged Business accounts can lose API access (official) and unofficial numbers get banned faster. This is the dominant existential risk, not a cost line.

Unofficial-API ToS violation: using whatsapp-web.js/Baileys violates WhatsApp ToS and risks number bans at any time; Meta can change the unofficial web protocol and break the stack with no notice. Treating $6/number/mo as a stable cost understates real ban-churn cost.

Anthropic pricing is cache-dated (2026-05-26) and not freshly verified at today's date (2026-06-10); the Meta official pricing page failed to fetch (truncated), so US rates rely on secondary aggregators — re-verify both before pricing decisions.
Per-message cost is country-dependent and can vary 10–60x; a per-worker price set on US rates will under-recover for EU-heavy worker bases (utility €0.0456, marketing up to €0.1365).
BSP lock-in and markup variability: WATI's +20% and Twilio's dual send+receive $0.005 can quietly double messaging cost vs 360dialog's flat $0.005 — BSP choice materially changes the model.
Token/turn estimates for AI are assumptions, not measured; real RAG/agent workloads with large context or tool-use loops can be 3–10x the baseline token count, making AI the runaway cost.
Residential/mobile proxy minimum spends and per-GB minimums (e.g. Smartproxy $75 min) impose a floor that makes the self-host approach uneconomic below ~hundreds of workers.
recommendations: Default to the OFFICIAL WhatsApp Business Platform via a flat-markup BSP (360dialog: $49/mo + $0.005/msg) rather than the unofficial stack — the ban/legal risk and ops churn of unofficial numbers outweigh the per-message savings for a B2B product, especially for adult-services accounts where account loss is catastrophic.

Architect messaging to keep utility replies INSIDE the free 24-hour customer service window — since July 2025 this makes most utility traffic free and collapses official messaging cost to roughly marketing-only. This is the highest-leverage cost lever available.

Use Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5 per 1M) for routine/classification/auto-reply turns and reserve Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15) or Opus 4.8 ($5/$25) for complex turns; route by complexity. Apply prompt caching (read ~0.1x) for the large stable system prompt and use the Batch API (-50%) for any non-realtime jobs. This is the main way to keep per-worker AI cost near $0.15–0.50.

Price the subscription tiered by scale and value, not flat: launch at ~$19–25/worker/month for small deployments (covers the $50–100 infra floor, ops/support, ban-recovery reserve, and ~70%+ margin), with volume tiers stepping to ~$9–15/worker at 10k+. At 100k workers a ~$12 price on ~$1.30–2.00 cost gives ~85% gross margin and room for support.

Build an explicit ban-recovery / number-warming reserve into the cost model (suggest $1–3/worker/month notional) rather than treating messaging numbers as zero-churn — this is the cost the headline per-message and per-number figures hide.

Re-verify ALL load-bearing prices against live sources before committing: Anthropic pricing page (cache is 2026-05-26), Meta official WhatsApp per-message rates for your actual country mix, and your chosen BSP's current markup. Set the per-worker price on a blended country rate, not US-only.

Model two messaging-mix scenarios (window-heavy/service-led vs marketing-heavy/out-of-window) explicitly — the per-worker official cost ranges from ~$0.50 to $5–15 depending entirely on the mix, and the price floor should be set against the worse case.

findings.summary: For a solo non-expert builder driving Claude, the pragmatic stack is Next.js + TypeScript on Vercel for the web/admin/API layer, Postgres via Supabase for data + auth, and a SEPARATE long-running Node.js worker service (on Fly.io/Railway/Render or a VPS) that holds the WhatsApp session and runs a Redis-backed job queue (BullMQ). The chat layer CANNOT live in serverless functions: Vercel Functions cap out at 300s (Hobby) / 800s (Pro) per request and explicitly do NOT support acting as a WebSocket server, while WhatsApp libraries (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js) require a persistent 24/7 WebSocket session that survives across requests — so it must run as an always-on process. CRITICAL non-technical risk for this specific use case: unofficial WhatsApp libraries violate Meta's ToS and accounts using them typically get permanently banned within 2-8 weeks; separately, Meta's official WhatsApp Business/Commerce Policy explicitly prohibits escort/sexual/adult services, so even the official API is not a compliant channel for adult-services content — this is a load-bearing product risk, not just an engineering detail. For secrets: .gitignore alone is insufficient because (a) it does not stop AI assistants reading local .env files and pasting them into committed code, and (b) The Register confirmed (28 Jan 2026, Claude Code v2.1.12) that Claude Code reads .env despite .gitignore/.claudeignore — the working mitigation is a permissions deny rule in .claude/settings.json ("Read(./.env)", "Read(./.env.*)", "Read(./secrets/**)"), backed by a gitleaks pre-commit hook as the final net, and a managed secrets store (Doppler, Infisical, SOPS, or the cloud provider's secret manager). Multi-tenant isolation should use a tenant_id/org_id column with Postgres Row-Level Security enabled on EVERY table, the user's JWT (anon key) for user-facing queries, and the service-role key only for trusted server jobs. Recommended simplest secure-by-default setup: a single monorepo (pnpm workspaces, optionally Turborepo) with apps/web + apps/worker + packages/shared, a basic GitHub Actions CI (lint + tsc --noEmit + tests) on every PR, gitleaks pre-commit + CI, RLS-on-by-default, and one secrets manager from day one.

keyFacts.fact: Vercel Functions cannot act as a WebSocket server and have a hard max request duration; this is why a persistent WhatsApp session cannot live in serverless functions
keyFacts.specifics: Vercel KB explicitly: 'Vercel Functions do not support acting as a WebSocket server.' Node.js function max duration: Hobby 300s (5 min, not configurable higher), Pro/Enterprise default 300s configurable up to 800s (~13 min). Request/response body cap 4.5 MB. Docs last updated 2026-06-02.

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp session libraries (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js) are WebSocket-based and require an always-on process; standard architecture is a dedicated VPS/worker running the session 24/7 with PM2/equivalent, while serverless only handles HTTP business logic
keyFacts.specifics: WhiskeySockets/Baileys is a socket-based TS API for WhatsApp Web; whatsapp-web.js current docs version 1.34.7. Recommended deploy = persistent Node VM, not serverless.

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial WhatsApp libraries violate Meta's Terms of Service and lead to account bans; this is a major product risk for the chat layer
keyFacts.specifics: Reported account lifespan ~2-8 weeks before permanent ban when using unofficial tools (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js, Evolution API, WA-Automate); automation-detection bans are typically permanent. The only ToS-compliant path is the official WhatsApp Business API via a registered BSP.

keyFacts.fact: Meta's official WhatsApp Business/Commerce Policy explicitly prohibits escort, prostitution, and sexual/adult services — so even the official API is not a compliant channel for adult-services content
keyFacts.specifics: Commerce policy: 'may not promote any form of human trafficking, prostitution, escort or sexual services'; messages must not contain 'sexually explicit materials or nudity'. Adult content is a high-risk category that triggers escalating messaging restrictions and offboarding.

keyFacts.fact: Claude Code reads .env files despite .gitignore and .claudeignore; only the settings.json permissions deny rule reliably blocks it
keyFacts.specifics: The Register, 28 Jan 2026, reproduced with Claude Code v2.1.12: .claudeignore and .gitignore did NOT block reads; .claude/settings.json deny rule did ('blocked by permission settings'). Risk amplified by indirect prompt injection for tool-enabled agents.

keyFacts.fact: Claude Code deny-rule syntax for protecting secrets from the model
keyFacts.specifics: In .claude/settings.json: {"permissions":{"deny":["Read(./.env)","Read(./.env.*)","Read(./secrets/**)"]}}. Deny rules take precedence over allow; * matches single level, ** recursive; rules merge across managed/project/user/local scopes; hot-reload, no OS-level sandbox.

keyFacts.fact: gitleaks is the standard pre-commit + CI secret scanner and is the most effective last-line defense against committing secrets
keyFacts.specifics: .pre-commit-config.yaml repo https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks, hook id: gitleaks (e.g. rev v8.24.2); blocks commits on detection; bypass with SKIP=gitleaks. Run in both pre-commit hook and CI.

keyFacts.fact: Doppler pricing (managed, proprietary, cannot self-host)
keyFacts.specifics: Developer: free up to 3 users then $8/user/mo; Team: $21/user/mo (14-day trial), ~$12/user/mo billed annually; Enterprise: custom. Team adds RBAC, SSO, 90-day activity logs, secret rotation.

keyFacts.fact: Infisical pricing (open-source MIT, self-hostable, identity-based billing)
keyFacts.specifics: Free: $0, up to 5 identities / 3 projects / 3 environments / 10 integrations. Pro: $18/mo per identity, unlimited identities+projects, up to 12 environments / 50 integrations, adds versioning, RBAC, SAML SSO, 90-day audit logs. Identities count both humans AND machines (CI, prod apps).

keyFacts.fact: SOPS is the free open-source option for committing encrypted secrets to git
keyFacts.specifics: Mozilla-origin, now CNCF; encrypts with AWS/GCP/Azure KMS or age/PGP; no server, no UI, no audit logs or dynamic secrets out of the box. Best for static config; pairs well with cloud KMS.

keyFacts.fact: Supabase enforces tenant isolation via Postgres Row-Level Security; RLS must be enabled on every table or the table is publicly readable via the API
keyFacts.specifics: Pattern: org_id/tenant_id column + RLS policies matching auth.jwt() claim. USING filters reads, WITH CHECK validates writes. Index RLS columns. service_role key BYPASSES RLS — use anon key + user JWT for user requests, service key only for trusted jobs. SQL Editor bypasses RLS, so test from the client SDK.

keyFacts.fact: Supabase pricing and free-tier gotcha (free projects pause)
keyFacts.specifics: Free $0 (2 active projects, 500 MB DB each, 50k MAU, 5 GB bandwidth, paused after 7 days inactivity, data retained). Pro $25/org/mo incl. $10 compute credit (one Micro), daily backups, no pausing. Team $599/org/mo. Each extra project from ~$10/mo compute.

keyFacts.fact: Serverless + Postgres requires a connection pooler in transaction mode
keyFacts.specifics: Supabase provides Supavisor/PgBouncer; for Prisma from serverless use transaction-mode connection string with ?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=1 (disables prepared statements). Persistent worker can use a direct/session connection.

keyFacts.fact: BullMQ (Redis-backed) is the standard Node queue for the worker layer
keyFacts.specifics: Provides retries, rate limits, delays, concurrency control. Redis via Upstash plans roughly $10/mo (250MB) to $400/mo (50GB); Railway/Render/Fly bill per service (web + db + redis + worker = separate charges).

keyFacts.fact: Monorepo is the recommended simplest structure for a solo builder of a 3-layer app
keyFacts.specifics: Solo/small fullstack should use monorepo with pnpm workspaces (+ optional Turborepo for caching task runs across packages); enables shared types/schemas and single PR across web+worker. Turborepo (by Vercel) practical for 5-50 packages. 3 separate repos add coordination overhead with little benefit at solo scale.

keyFacts.fact: Minimal solo CI on GitHub Actions
keyFacts.specifics: Run on PR: ESLint, tsc --noEmit (type-check), tests, npm audit/gitleaks; ubuntu-latest, Node 20, cache deps, concurrency cancel-in-progress. GitHub Actions pricing dropped ~39% in Jan 2026.

loadBearingClaims: Vercel Functions cannot act as a WebSocket server and cap request duration at 300s (Hobby)/800s (Pro), so a persistent WhatsApp session must run as a separate always-on worker process, not in serverless functions.

Unofficial WhatsApp libraries (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js) violate Meta ToS and reliably get accounts permanently banned (often within 2-8 weeks), making them an unstable foundation for a production chat layer.

Meta's official WhatsApp Commerce/Business Policy explicitly prohibits escort/prostitution/sexual/adult services, so neither the unofficial libraries NOR the official Business API is a sanctioned channel for adult-services content — this constrains the whole product, not just one component.

Claude Code reads .env files despite .gitignore/.claudeignore (confirmed by The Register, Jan 2026, v2.1.12); only a .claude/settings.json permissions deny rule reliably blocks the model from reading secrets, so that deny rule + gitleaks pre-commit are must-haves from day one.
Supabase tenant isolation depends on RLS being enabled on every table and the service-role key (which bypasses RLS) never being used for user-facing requests.
risks: WhatsApp ban + ToS: unofficial libraries get accounts permanently banned (2-8 weeks typical); business continuity of the entire chat product depends on a non-compliant channel. Mitigate by designing the messaging layer to be provider-swappable (abstract behind an internal interface) and by evaluating whether SMS/Signal/Telegram or a compliant channel better fits the user base.

Adult-services content is explicitly prohibited by Meta's WhatsApp Commerce/Business Policy and is a high-risk category for escalating restrictions/offboarding — using WhatsApp for adult-services workflows risks losing the channel AND the linked Meta business assets. This is a legal/policy risk that should be assessed before building on WhatsApp at all.

AI-assistant secret leakage: .gitignore does NOT stop Claude/Cursor from reading local .env and pasting secrets into committed code; without the settings.json deny rule + gitleaks, keys can reach git or the model's context. Indirect prompt injection can coax a tool-enabled agent into exfiltrating secrets.

Supabase service_role key bypasses RLS entirely; a single misuse in a user-facing route exposes all tenants' data. The SQL Editor also bypasses RLS, so policies that 'look fine' there can still leak from the client.
Serverless DB connection exhaustion: without transaction-mode pooling (?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=1), Next.js serverless functions can exhaust Postgres connections under load.

Free-tier surprises: Supabase free projects pause after 7 days inactivity (bad for an always-needed worker); the worker layer and Redis must be on an always-on paid host, not a free serverless/paused tier.

Solo-builder single point of failure: one person + AI means review gaps; mandatory CI gates (type-check, tests, secret scan) and RLS-on-by-default are the cheapest guardrails against shipping a data leak.

Highly sensitive PII (sex-worker client data): a breach has outsized real-world harm. Beyond RLS, consider encryption-at-rest for sensitive columns, strict audit logging, minimal data retention, and not storing message content longer than needed.
recommendations: Adopt a monorepo: pnpm workspaces with apps/web (Next.js + TS on Vercel), apps/worker (always-on Node service holding the chat session + BullMQ), packages/shared (types, DB schema, validation). Add Turborepo only if build/test caching becomes slow.

Host the chat/worker layer on an always-on platform (Fly.io, Railway, Render, or a small VPS with PM2) with a managed Redis (Upstash or provider Redis) for BullMQ. Do NOT put the WhatsApp session in Vercel functions.

Treat the messaging channel as swappable: put WhatsApp behind an internal MessagingProvider interface so you can move to SMS/Signal/Telegram or an official BSP without rewriting the app. Get legal/policy advice on Meta's adult-services prohibition before committing to WhatsApp.

Secrets day-one setup (simplest secure default): (1) gitignore .env and all AI tool dirs; (2) add .claude/settings.json deny rules for Read(./.env), Read(./.env.*), Read(./secrets/**); (3) gitleaks as a pre-commit hook AND a CI job; (4) one secrets manager — Infisical free tier or Doppler free Developer tier for a solo builder, or SOPS+cloud KMS if you prefer git-committed encrypted secrets; (5) inject secrets at runtime via the host's env/secret manager, never hard-code.

For multi-tenancy: single Postgres with a tenant_id/org_id column, RLS enabled on EVERY table (deny by default), policies keyed to auth.jwt(); use the anon key + user JWT for all user-facing queries and reserve the service_role key for trusted worker jobs only. Index every RLS column and test policies from the client SDK, not the SQL Editor.

Use transaction-mode pooling (Supabase Supavisor/PgBouncer) for the serverless web app — Prisma string with ?pgbouncer=true&connection_limit=1 — while the persistent worker can use a direct/session connection.

Minimal CI from commit #1: a GitHub Actions workflow on every PR running eslint, tsc --noEmit, the test suite, and gitleaks, on Node 20 with dependency caching and concurrency cancel-in-progress. Start TDD on the security-critical paths (RLS-protected queries, secret handling, tenant scoping) even if you don't TDD everything.

Encrypt sensitive PII columns at rest, keep message/content retention minimal, enable audit logging, and budget for always-on paid hosting (Supabase Pro $25/mo to avoid project pausing + a worker host + Redis) rather than relying on free tiers that pause.
