findings.summary: A CRM serving adult-services workers sits at the intersection of three distinct, overlapping risk regimes: criminal facilitation law, payment-network/platform deplatforming, and data-protection liability for highly sensitive data. The legality of sex work itself varies enormously by jurisdiction — full decriminalisation (New Zealand 2003, parts of Australia: NSW, Victoria 2023, Queensland 2024), legalised-and-regulated (Germany, Netherlands), Nordic/"end demand" models that criminalise buyers AND third-party facilitation/"profiting" (Sweden, France, Ireland, Norway, Iceland, Northern Ireland), and near-total criminalisation (most of the US). The pivotal legal line for a tooling provider is the difference between neutral tooling and "knowingly facilitating": US FOSTA-SESTA (signed 11 April 2018) created 18 U.S.C. 2421A (up to 10 years prison; 25 years for an "aggravated" violation involving 5+ persons or reckless disregard of trafficking) and stripped Section 230 immunity for sex-trafficking facilitation, triggering broad pre-emptive deplatforming (Craigslist personals, Backpage seizure). The DC Circuit (Woodhull v US, 7 July 2023) upheld FOSTA but narrowed it: it does not reach general advocacy, education, or advice to sex workers for their protection — an important safe harbour for a harm-reduction tool. Payment networks (Visa/Mastercard) function as de facto regulators: mainstream processors (Stripe, PayPal) flatly prohibit adult/escort/sexual services in their terms, so any product that touches payment flow inherits sudden-freeze and account-termination risk (the OnlyFans Aug 2021 episode is the canonical example). The data itself is the gravest exposure: under GDPR/UK GDPR Article 9, data concerning a person's sex life or sexual orientation is "special category," and combining worker + client real identities with chat logs creates a doxxing/extortion/outing dataset whose breach can carry fines up to EUR 20M / 4% global turnover (UK: GBP 17.5M / 4%). WhatsApp/Meta's Business Platform prohibits adult/sexual services and end-to-end encryption stops at Meta's Cloud API (content is decrypted server-side; metadata is never E2E-encrypted), making it a poor channel and a poor data store for this use case. The defensible design pattern is: neutral general-purpose framing, aggressive data minimisation, no payment handling, worker-held encryption keys (zero-knowledge), and jurisdiction-aware hosting.

keyFacts.fact: US FOSTA-SESTA created 18 U.S.C. 2421A criminalising owning/managing/operating an interactive computer service with intent to promote or facilitate prostitution
keyFacts.specifics: Signed 11 April 2018; up to 10 years prison (standard); up to 25 years for 'aggravated' violation = facilitating prostitution of 5+ persons OR acting with reckless disregard that conduct contributed to sex trafficking; civil damages + mandatory restitution for aggravated violations

keyFacts.fact: FOSTA also amended 47 U.S.C. 230 (Section 230) so its immunity does not bar state criminal/civil enforcement targeting conduct that violates federal sex-trafficking law, and amended 18 U.S.C. 1591 to define 'participation in a venture' as 'knowingly assisting, supporting, or facilitating' a violation
keyFacts.specifics: The operative mens rea is 'knowingly' — neutral conduct without knowing facilitation is the key distinction

keyFacts.fact: The DC Circuit upheld FOSTA's constitutionality but adopted a narrowing construction protecting advocacy/education
keyFacts.specifics: Woodhull Freedom Foundation v. United States, No. 22-5105 (D.C. Cir.), decided 7 July 2023: FOSTA does NOT reach intent to engage in general advocacy about prostitution or to give advice to sex workers generally to protect them from abuse; not overbroad/vague

keyFacts.fact: FOSTA caused immediate pre-emptive deplatforming beyond its literal scope (the 'chilling effect')
keyFacts.specifics: Craigslist removed its Personals section US-wide ('Any tool or service can be misused. We can't take such risk.'); Backpage was seized by federal authorities days before signing; bad-date lists and screening tools went offline

keyFacts.fact: Empirical research suggests removing online screening platforms harmed worker safety
keyFacts.specifics: Baylor University study: rollout of Craigslist 'erotic services' was associated with a female homicide rate drop of roughly 1% to 17.6%; multiple 2018-2023 studies find FOSTA reduced income and screening access and increased violence risk, especially for trans/BIPOC workers

keyFacts.fact: Sex-work legality varies by jurisdiction across four model types, materially changing third-party/intermediary exposure
keyFacts.specifics: Full decrim: New Zealand (2003), NSW, Victoria (2023), Queensland (2024); legalise+regulate: Germany (Prostitutes Protection Act 2016), Netherlands; Nordic/'end demand' criminalising buyers AND third-party facilitation/'profiting': Sweden (1999), France (2016), Ireland (2017), Norway, Iceland, Northern Ireland; ECtHR upheld France's law (July 2024)

keyFacts.fact: Nordic-model jurisdictions criminalise third-party 'profiting'/facilitation even where selling sex is legal — a specific intermediary-tooling risk
keyFacts.specifics: Sweden criminalises 'profiting' from sex work; workers can't safely share premises, hire drivers/security; UK Sexual Offences Act 2003 ss.52-53 'causing/inciting' and 'controlling prostitution for gain' are routinely used against workers operating together

keyFacts.fact: The UK Online Safety Act 2023 lists 'causing, inciting or controlling prostitution for gain' (SOA 2003 ss.52-53) as priority illegal content platforms must remove
keyFacts.specifics: Non-compliance: fines up to GBP 18M or 10% of global turnover and potential jail for senior managers; advocates report platforms pre-emptively removing sex-worker ads

keyFacts.fact: Mainstream payment processors flatly prohibit adult/sexual services; networks act as de facto regulators
keyFacts.specifics: Stripe Restricted Businesses (last updated 2026-05-13) prohibits 'prostitution, escorts, pay-per-view, sexual massages, fetish services... adult live-chat' and pornography; PayPal similar; rationale cited as card-network/banking-partner rules. Visa increased High-Risk merchant registration fee from $500 to $950 per acquirer effective 1 April 2024

keyFacts.fact: Payment deplatforming risk is concrete and sudden — OnlyFans is the canonical case
keyFacts.specifics: Aug 2021 OnlyFans announced an explicit-content ban citing banks (named BNY Mellon, Metro Bank, JPMorgan Chase), reversed within ~1 week after banking-partner assurances; Mastercard tightened adult-content rules April 2021 (mandatory age/ID verification)

keyFacts.fact: Worker+client real-identity data plus chat logs is GDPR/UK GDPR Article 9 'special category' data (sex life / sexual orientation) and carries the highest fine tier
keyFacts.specifics: GDPR max EUR 20M or 4% global annual turnover; UK ICO max GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover; client real identities can also be criminal-offence-related data (Art.10 / DPA 2018 s.11). Special category processing requires an Art.9(2) condition (e.g. explicit consent)

keyFacts.fact: Australia's reformed Privacy Act treats 'sexual orientation or practices' as sensitive information and added new enforcement teeth
keyFacts.specifics: Privacy and Other Legislation Amendment Act 2024 passed 29 Nov 2024: statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy; OAIC infringement notices up to AUD 66,000 per contravention from Dec 2024; mandatory Notifiable Data Breach reporting where 'serious harm' likely (2024 saw 1,113 breaches reported, a record)

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp Business Platform prohibits adult/sexual services and its E2E encryption does NOT cover the business-API data path
keyFacts.specifics: Adult content is a high-risk policy category; enforcement escalates via 1/3/5/7-day messaging blocks up to indefinite blocks. Via the Cloud API, messages are decrypted at Meta's endpoint before forwarding to the business; metadata (phone numbers, timestamps, frequency) is never E2E-encrypted

keyFacts.fact: Current security 'state of the art' baseline (Art.32) for sensitive data
keyFacts.specifics: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, keys held in HSM/dedicated key vault separated from ciphertext, RBAC + MFA; data minimisation explicitly reduces breach exposure

loadBearingClaims: The legal hinge for a tooling provider is the 'neutral tooling vs knowingly facilitating' line: US FOSTA-SESTA (18 U.S.C. 2421A) requires intent to promote/facilitate prostitution and 'knowing' assistance; a general-purpose CRM that does not advertise, broker, or knowingly facilitate specific prostitution transactions is materially less exposed than a sex-work-branded brokering platform.

Woodhull v. US (DC Cir., 7 July 2023) narrowed FOSTA so it does not reach general advocacy, education, or advice to sex workers for their protection — supporting a harm-reduction / neutral-tooling framing as a defensible posture (though this is a federal appellate construction of intent, not a guarantee against prosecution).

Touching payment flow for adult services is the single fastest route to deplatforming: mainstream processors (Stripe, PayPal) prohibit it outright in their terms, so a 'no payment handling' design removes a whole class of account-freeze and contractual-termination risk.

Centralising worker + client real identities + chat logs creates Article 9 special-category data whose breach is the highest-fine tier (EUR 20M/4%; UK GBP 17.5M/4%) AND a doxxing/outing/extortion dataset; data minimisation and worker-held (zero-knowledge) keys move the provider from 'holds a catastrophic dataset' toward 'cannot read it,' reducing both breach blast radius and the provider's exposure to compelled disclosure.

Nordic-model and UK third-party 'facilitation'/'profiting'/'controlling for gain' offences criminalise intermediaries even where selling sex is legal — so jurisdiction-aware framing and hosting matter, and 'legal where the worker is' is not sufficient if the provider could be read as facilitating in a Nordic-model state.
risks: Criminal facilitation exposure: in the US (FOSTA/2421A) and Nordic-model jurisdictions, a provider that knowingly facilitates specific prostitution transactions (brokering, advertising, taking a cut) risks criminal liability up to 25 years (US aggravated) and loss of Section 230 protection; the line between 'tool' and 'facilitator' turns on knowledge and intent, which marketing and feature choices can inadvertently establish.

Payment-network deplatforming: any payment handling triggers Stripe/PayPal/Visa/Mastercard prohibitions; sudden account closure and fund freezes (OnlyFans precedent) can be existential and are largely outside the provider's control.

Catastrophic data-breach harm: a breach of a centralised worker+client identity + chat-log store is not just a fine (up to EUR 20M/4%; GBP 17.5M/4%; AUD penalties + new statutory privacy tort) but a direct safety threat — outing, stalking, extortion, and exposure to violence for vulnerable users.

Compelled disclosure / law-enforcement access: holding plaintext data in a criminalising jurisdiction exposes the provider to subpoenas/warrants that could expose clients and workers; if the provider can read the data, it can be forced to produce it.

Platform-channel ToS: building on WhatsApp/Meta exposes the product to adult-content policy enforcement (escalating blocks to permanent ban) and to the fact that Meta's Cloud API decrypts content server-side and never encrypts metadata — undermining any privacy promise made to users.

Jurisdictional whack-a-mole and conflict of laws: a single global product can be lawful tooling in NZ/decriminalised Australia yet facilitation in Sweden/France/most of the US; sources sometimes conflate 'legal/decriminalised/regulated' and use shifting effective dates, so jurisdiction mapping must be done per-feature, not per-country.

Sources used here are a mix of primary (statutes, ICO/OAIC, Stripe terms, court opinions) and secondary/advocacy commentary; the empirical safety-benefit figures (e.g. Craigslist homicide study) are contested and should be treated as indicative, not settled.
recommendations: Frame and build as neutral, general-purpose tooling (a privacy-first CRM/scheduling/contacts tool that any service worker can use), not a sex-work brokering or advertising platform; avoid any feature that advertises specific sexual services, brokers transactions, or takes a per-booking cut — these are the facts that establish 'knowing facilitation' under FOSTA/2421A and Nordic-model facilitation offences.

Handle no payments at all: do not be in the money flow, do not store card data, do not act as a marketplace-of-record. This removes Stripe/PayPal/Visa/Mastercard prohibition exposure and the OnlyFans-style freeze risk in one decision.

Minimise data ruthlessly: do not require or centralise client real identities; prefer worker-chosen aliases/handles, short retention windows, local-first storage, and avoid storing chat logs server-side. Under GDPR data-minimisation and Art.9, the safest special-category data is the data you never collect.

Use worker-held / client-side (zero-knowledge) encryption so the provider cannot read worker or client identities or notes (keys on the worker's device, not on central servers, in an HSM/key-vault if any server keys exist). This shrinks breach blast radius and limits compelled-disclosure exposure — you cannot hand over plaintext you cannot decrypt.

Adopt jurisdiction-aware hosting and feature-gating: map each feature against the four legality models, host data in privacy-protective/decriminalised jurisdictions where lawful, and gate or disable features (especially anything resembling facilitation/'profiting'/'controlling for gain') in Nordic-model and criminalising jurisdictions; document this as a risk-tiered matrix.

Do not build on WhatsApp/Meta as the core channel or data store for sensitive content: Meta's Business Platform prohibits adult services (escalating to permanent ban) and decrypts content at the Cloud API with no E2E on metadata. If a messaging channel is needed, prefer genuinely E2E options under worker control, and never centralise message contents.

Lean on the Woodhull narrowing: position the product explicitly around safety, screening, harm reduction, and advice/education for workers' protection — the conduct the DC Circuit said FOSTA does NOT reach — and keep clear internal documentation of that purpose. (This is risk-awareness, not legal advice; obtain qualified counsel in each target jurisdiction before launch.)

Engineer to current Art.32 baselines (TLS 1.3, AES-256, RBAC, MFA, key separation), run a DPIA given the special-category data, and prepare breach-response under the relevant Notifiable Data Breach / GDPR 72-hour regimes before go-live, not after.

findings.summary: The core goals conflict: full-history plus indistinguishable-from-a-person plus never-banned cannot coexist on any sanctioned path, so durable odds are low (5 to 15 percent), and building shared infra first maximizes the cascade-ban the user fears.

keyFacts.fact: Unofficial WhatsApp libraries (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js) violate ToS and get banned even after years; survival is typically 2 to 8 weeks
keyFacts.specifics: Baileys issue 1869 reports 5 bots banned in one week and two 3-year accounts suddenly terminated

keyFacts.fact: Official Cloud API cannot bulk-import full personal history; only Coexistence syncs a limited window
keyFacts.specifics: Coexistence launched May 6 2025, up to about 6 months, no group chats on API, open app every 13 days, capped 20 msg per sec

keyFacts.fact: WhatsApp policy explicitly prohibits escorting, prostitution and sexual services
keyFacts.specifics: Repeat high-risk violations lead to permanent termination

keyFacts.fact: Meta anti-spam ML clusters accounts by shared fingerprint, IP and behavior and batch-bans them
keyFacts.specifics: Once one account is flagged, related accounts get suspended; the exact multi-tenant threat model

keyFacts.fact: Off-the-shelf WhatsApp CRMs already provide shared inbox, tagging, CRM sync and automation
keyFacts.specifics: TimelinesAI 10 to 60, Rasayel 30 to 40 per seat, Callbell 15 to 20 per agent; none knowingly serve adult services

keyFacts.fact: US operator liability under FOSTA and SESTA uses a low reckless-disregard facilitation standard
keyFacts.specifics: Federal offense since 2018; Woodhull v US upheld DC Circuit 2023; Section 230 does not shield it

keyFacts.fact: Data is GDPR Article 9 special-category sex-life plus Article 10 criminal-flagged clients
keyFacts.specifics: Dual basis, explicit consent, mandatory DPIA; fines up to 20M EUR or 4 percent global turnover

keyFacts.fact: AI bot-disclosure laws conflict with AI negotiating while the client thinks it is the human worker
keyFacts.specifics: California SB 243 needs clear disclosure with private right of action; EU AI Act Article 50 phases in mid-2026

keyFacts.fact: Recycled or reassigned burner numbers create identity-takeover risk
keyFacts.specifics: 66 percent of recycled US numbers still link to a prior owner; about 35M recycled annually; no warning

keyFacts.fact: Cloud API per-message pricing after July 1 2025 makes AI volume metered not flat
keyFacts.specifics: Marketing about 0.01 to 0.14 USD per message; US about 0.025; service-window non-template messages free

loadBearingClaims: Full history plus a human-like presence force an unofficial bridge Meta bans; the official API offers neither, so the three goals cannot coexist on a sanctioned path.
Meta batch-bans accounts sharing device, IP and behavior fingerprints, so a multi-tenant shared-infra platform raises the cascade-ban risk the user fears most.
The policy bans escorting, so no compliant WhatsApp CRM can be bought or resold for this exact case; build-vs-buy is moot on the legal axis.
FOSTA and SESTA reckless-disregard facilitation exposes the operator personally, not just the worker, in the US.
Bot-disclosure laws (SB 243, EU AI Act) make the flagship secretly-automate-negotiation feature potentially unlawful in key markets.
risks: Premature architecture: the 3-layer split optimizes multi-tenancy and pluggability with zero users while answering none of the kill question.
Wrong de-risking order: shared infra first builds a central DB linking workers and clients; one breach exposes both. Build the bridge first, the DB last.
Cascade ban: one burner triggers fingerprint linkage and every tenant is banned at once with no graceful degradation.
On ban, all live client threads strand with no fallback and dangerous-client tags vanish exactly when needed.
Operator legal exposure (FOSTA/SESTA, GDPR Art 9/10, bot-disclosure) can attach to the solo builder personally.

Unmentioned edge cases: client also messaging the worker real personal WhatsApp; phone-and-app simultaneous use causing session conflicts; groups unsupported on API; voice notes and calls; read receipts, typing and presence revealing bot timing.
Recycled-number takeover hands a stranger the worker identity and inbound client messages, a physical-safety risk.
GDPR subject-access, deletion and worker-churn export are unsolved by an encrypted-DB-plus-key-pairs hand-wave.
One test worker hides the multi-tenant detection dynamics that only emerge at more than one tenant.
recommendations: Falsify the premise first: run one throwaway number through an off-the-shelf bridge with human-paced traffic for about 2 weeks and measure time-to-ban; write no platform code until it passes.
Invert build order: de-risk WhatsApp survivability, then the CRM app, and build the shared central DB and multi-tenant infra last or never.
Build-vs-buy honestly: wrap TimelinesAI, Rasayel or Callbell for a generic version; the adult case is blocked for policy and legal reasons, signalling a fragile model.
Reconsider channel: Telegram (sanctioned bot API, no adult-content ban) or Signal may meet the harm-reduction goals more cheaply than WhatsApp.
Get a legal opinion first: FOSTA/SESTA by US state, GDPR Art 9/10 DPIA, bot-disclosure for the AI, and call-recording consent in two-party-consent areas.
Design for ban-as-normal: assume accounts die; keep CRM data and tags decoupled from the WhatsApp session so a ban never strands bookings.
Relabel AI as a clearly-disclosed assistant, not secretly-the-worker, to stay compatible with SB 243 and the EU AI Act.

verdicts.claim: The ONLY way to preserve a worker's pre-existing chats/contacts is Meta Coexistence, and even then it is capped at ~6 months of 1:1 text + 2 weeks of media, with NO group chats and NO data older than that imported — a full lifetime history import is impossible.
verdicts.explanation:
The technical limits in the claim are well-corroborated by multiple independent WhatsApp Business Solution Providers (BSPs) implementing against Meta's API, and the structural claim that a full lifetime import into the platform is impossible holds up. But the absolutist framing ("the ONLY way to preserve") is overstated and the word "import" is imprecise.

CONFIRMED parts:
- ~6 months / 180 days of 1:1 text history: Confirmed. ChakraHQ and 360dialog describe the history sync as up to 180 days, delivered in phases (0-1 day, 1-90 days, 90-180 days). Wati states "Chat history from the past 6 months can be synced."
- 2 weeks / 14 days of media: Confirmed. ChakraHQ ("media from the last 14 days syncs separately") and PickyAssist/SalesHiker corroborate the 14-day media window; media older than 14 days does not sync.
- NO group chats: Confirmed and consistent across all sources. Group chats (plus disappearing/view-once messages, broadcast lists, calls) remain app-only and are not mirrored to the API. (Note: Meta launched a separate native Cloud API Group Chat capability around Oct 2025, but it is gated to very high-volume senders and explicitly NOT available to Coexistence accounts, so it does not change this.)
- NO older data / lifetime import impossible: Confirmed for the in-platform path. Nothing beyond the 180-day text / 14-day media windows is synced into the Cloud API / CRM. Meta provides no bulk historical-import API for pre-existing WhatsApp Business App chats. A direct (non-Coexistence) migration to Cloud API destroys the app history entirely (multiple sources state existing messaging history "will be lost" / "permanently removed" and there is "no option to import previous conversations").

OVERSTATED / NEEDS CORRECTION parts:
1. "the ONLY way to preserve" — Coexistence is the only Meta-native way to keep prior chats accessible while moving to the API, but it is not literally the only way to PRESERVE the data. Workers/businesses can also (a) keep full lifetime history on the device/app because Coexistence does not delete the WhatsApp Business App — the app retains everything locally; and (b) export chats (HTML/media, or via tools CSV/Excel) into a CRM. These are genuine preservation methods, just not via the API sync. So "only way to preserve" should be narrowed to "only Meta-native way to migrate to the API while keeping prior conversations visible across both surfaces."
2. "imported" is imprecise — Coexistence does not truly "import a worker's lifetime history into the CRM and then cap it." Rather, the FULL history stays on the phone in the Business App; only a capped window (180d text / 14d media, no groups) is synced/mirrored into the API/CRM. The cap is on what crosses into the API, not on what is preserved on-device.
3. The exact figures are sourced from BSP implementation docs rather than a directly machine-readable Meta page (Meta's developer docs are JS-rendered and did not return body text on fetch). The figures are consistent across 6+ independent BSPs, so confidence is high, but the precise numbers ultimately derive from Meta's spec and could change via changelog.

Net: every concrete number in the claim checks out; the claim is wrong only in its absolutism ("ONLY way to preserve") and its loose use of "imported" vs. "synced to the API."
verdicts.corrected: Meta Coexistence is the only Meta-native way to move a worker's number to the WhatsApp Cloud API while keeping their prior conversations visible across both the Business App and the API/CRM. When chat-history sync is enabled, only a capped window crosses into the API/CRM: roughly the last 6 months (180 days) of 1:1 text messages and the last 2 weeks (14 days) of media, with NO group chats, calls, disappearing/view-once messages, or broadcast lists, and nothing older than those windows. There is no bulk lifetime-history import into the platform. However, the worker's FULL lifetime history is not destroyed — it remains on the device in the WhatsApp Business App (Coexistence does not delete it), and it can also be preserved outside the API by exporting chats/media or importing them into a CRM. By contrast, a direct (non-Coexistence) migration to the Cloud API does permanently erase the app's chat history. So the limits on what is synced into the API are accurate, but "the only way to preserve" and "lifetime history import is impossible" overstate the case: lifetime history survives on-device and via export; only the in-platform/API sync is capped.

verdicts.claim: Coexistence requires the worker to already be on the WhatsApp BUSINESS app (green), not the consumer WhatsApp app; a worker using only personal/consumer WhatsApp cannot use Coexistence and would lose all history if they migrate.
verdicts.explanation:
The claim has two parts; the first is correct, the second is misleading.

PART 1 - CONFIRMED. WhatsApp Coexistence (Meta calls it "Onboarding WhatsApp Business app users") lets a single number run on the WhatsApp Business app AND the Cloud API simultaneously. It works ONLY with the WhatsApp Business app, not the consumer/personal app (WhatsApp Messenger). Requirements include WhatsApp Business app v2.24.17+, a verified Meta/Facebook Business Manager, real conversation history, and no active bans. So a worker on consumer-only WhatsApp genuinely cannot enable Coexistence directly; they must first convert/switch to the WhatsApp Business app. Meta's own docs state messaging history is preserved when onboarding an existing Business-app number into Coexistence (up to ~6 months of 1:1 chat history syncs to the API; groups, disappearing/view-once messages do not sync). Feature was introduced via the Embedded Signup update on 2025-05-06 and has since gone broadly available, though Coexistence is NOT supported for numbers in many regions (commonly cited: EU/EEA, UK, Australia +61, Japan +81, South Korea +82, Philippines +63, Russia +7, Turkey +90, Switzerland +41, Nigeria +234, South Africa +27) - this varies by source/date, so check current Meta docs for the worker's country.

PART 2 - REFUTED / INACCURATE. The phrase "would lose all history if they migrate" is wrong as a general statement. Per WhatsApp's official Help Center, switching from personal/consumer WhatsApp (Messenger) to the WhatsApp Business app on the SAME phone using the SAME number carries over chat history and account information; the standard switch flow uses a local/cloud backup-and-restore so conversations, media and contacts are retained. History loss only occurs in mishandled cases (no backup taken, switching to a different number, or cross-OS moves Android<->iPhone where backup/restore is unreliable). So conversion from consumer to Business app does NOT inherently destroy history - the claim overstates the risk.

CONFLICTS/UNCERTAINTY: Most detail comes from BSP/third-party docs (ycloud, msg91, respond.io, 360dialog, etc.) because Meta's developer pages returned truncated content via fetch; however a Meta-doc search excerpt explicitly states "your messaging history will be preserved" for Coexistence onboarding, and the consumer-app-not-supported point is consistent across all sources. The exact list of unsupported countries and the precise history-sync window (6 months) should be re-verified against current Meta docs for a given worker.
verdicts.corrected: Coexistence works only with the WhatsApp Business app (v2.24.17+), not the consumer/personal WhatsApp app, so a worker on consumer-only WhatsApp cannot enable Coexistence directly and must first switch to the WhatsApp Business app. However, switching is NOT history-destroying: converting personal WhatsApp to the WhatsApp Business app on the same phone with the same number preserves chat history (via the standard backup-and-restore flow). History is only lost if the switch is mishandled (no backup, different number, or an unreliable cross-OS move). After switching, enabling Coexistence then syncs up to ~6 months of 1:1 chat history to the API. (Coexistence is also unavailable for numbers in certain regions, e.g. EU/EEA/UK and several other countries - verify per the worker's country.)

verdicts.claim: Per-message pricing replaced conversation-based pricing effective July 1, 2025.
verdicts.explanation: Verified against Meta's primary documentation and multiple official Business Solution Provider (BSP) sources, all of which consistently date the change to July 1, 2025. Meta's developer documentation page for conversation-based pricing is now explicitly titled "Conversation-based pricing (Deprecated)," and the active WhatsApp Business Platform pricing page describes a per-message (per delivered template message) model. Twilio's changelog quotes Meta directly: "Starting July 1, 2025, Meta will shift from 24-hour conversation-based pricing to a new model that charges per template message delivered." YCloud, Gupshup, AiChat, CleverTap and others independently confirm the same July 1, 2025 effective date. Under the new model, businesses are charged only when a template message is delivered, with rates varying by template category (Marketing/Utility/Authentication) and recipient country code; non-template (session) messages and utility templates sent within an open 24-hour customer service window are free. I actively tried to refute the date and found two nuances that ENRICH but do not contradict the claim: (1) Meta had originally signaled an earlier transition (some BSP trackers reference a ~May 1, 2025 target) that was postponed to July 1, 2025, so July 1 is the actual, not merely announced, effective date; and (2) there were earlier intermediate steps before the full model change — service conversations became free (Nov 1, 2024) and utility templates within the customer service window became free around April 2025 — but the complete deprecation of conversation-based pricing in favor of per-message pricing landed on July 1, 2025. July 2025 was a hybrid billing month (messages billed under whichever model applied at delivery time), but the cutover date is unambiguously July 1, 2025. I could not retrieve the full rendered text of Meta's own pages via the fetch tool (the developer.facebook.com pages returned truncated/JS-gated content and MEF/Gupshup returned 403), so the strongest direct quote is Twilio's verbatim restatement of Meta's notice; however, the agreement across Meta's deprecation page title plus every independent BSP makes the July 1, 2025 date high-confidence.

verdicts.claim: Marketing is always paid; Authentication is always paid; Utility is free only within an open 24h customer service window and paid outside it; Service messages are free and unlimited since Nov 1, 2024.
verdicts.explanation: All four sub-claims hold against primary and BSP-partner sources as of 2026-06-10. WhatsApp's per-message model (in force since July 1, 2025, replacing conversation-based pricing) uses four categories: Marketing, Utility, Authentication, Service. (1) Marketing templates are charged per message sent with no in-window exemption — confirmed by Twilio (a Meta Business Solution Provider) and respond.io. (2) Authentication templates are charged per message sent — Twilio lists a per-message Meta fee (e.g. ~$0.0034) and respond.io states they are 'charged per message sent.' (3) Utility templates are free when delivered within an open 24-hour customer service window (CSW) and charged at the standard utility rate when sent outside it — Twilio: 'During a customer service window, Meta does not charge for utility template messages or free-form messages'; this free-in-window rule became standard for all businesses on July 1, 2025. (4) Service messages/conversations are free and unlimited since November 1, 2024, when Meta removed the prior cap of 1,000 free service conversations per month — confirmed by respond.io and 8x8 CPaaS. Caveats: the per-message structure took effect July 1, 2025 (before that, conversation-based pricing applied); rates vary by country; there is also a separate 'free entry point' window (ads/Facebook-page click-to-WhatsApp) that can make even template messages free for 72 hours, which is an additional exemption not captured by the claim. Meta's own developer docs (developers.facebook.com) are JavaScript-rendered and returned truncated content to the fetch tool, so corroboration relies on Twilio (Meta BSP) plus multiple secondary sources that quote Meta directly and agree with each other.

verdicts.claim: The Meta Commerce Policy contains the verbatim prohibition on 'human trafficking, prostitution, escort or sexual services' and on sexually suggestive adult products, and this Commerce Policy governs WhatsApp commerce/catalog use.
verdicts.explanation:
I attempted to refute this claim using Meta and WhatsApp primary sources and could not; both load-bearing parts hold as of 2026-06-10.

PART 1 - Verbatim sexual-services prohibition (CONFIRMED, exact match): The Meta Commerce Policy (facebook.com/policies_center/commerce) contains the verbatim sentence: "Commerce content may not promote any form of human trafficking, prostitution, escort or sexual services." This appears under the policy's "Human Exploitation and Sexual Services" prohibited-content section. The claim's quoted fragment 'human trafficking, prostitution, escort or sexual services' is an exact word-for-word substring of the live policy text.

PART 2 - Adult / sexually suggestive products (CONFIRMED in substance; the claim's phrasing is a paraphrase, not a single verbatim quote): The policy prohibits adult products and sexually suggestive positioning via the wording "Commerce content may not promote the buying, selling, or trading of adult products and may not position products in a sexually suggestive manner." The claim compresses this into the phrase "sexually suggestive adult products." That is accurate as substance but is NOT a single verbatim string in the policy - the policy expresses it as two coordinated clauses (a ban on adult products, plus a ban on positioning any product in a sexually suggestive manner). This is the only nuance; it does not change the verdict.

PART 3 - Commerce Policy governs WhatsApp commerce/catalog (CONFIRMED, verbatim): The WhatsApp Business Policy (whatsappbusiness.com/policy/, canonical redirect target of business.whatsapp.com/policy) states verbatim: "The Commerce Policy is used to govern whether businesses can sell their products and services using WhatsApp Business Services, such as WhatsApp commerce product features like catalog and payments," and: "If you use Catalogs, or provide any other commerce experiences to sell or otherwise facilitate the exchange of goods or services prohibited by the Meta Commerce Policy, then we may prohibit you from using some or all of the WhatsApp Business Services." The Meta Commerce Policy itself also lists "WhatsApp Business Catalog" among the covered commerce surfaces. This directly establishes that the Meta Commerce Policy governs WhatsApp commerce/catalog use.

Refutation attempts that failed: (a) exact-phrase web search did not surface Meta's page (Meta policy pages are JS-rendered and poorly indexed for exact-quote search), but direct WebFetch of the live policy returned the exact text; (b) the WhatsApp Business Terms of Service (whatsapp.com/legal/business-terms) and Meta Terms for WhatsApp Business (whatsapp.com/legal/meta-terms-whatsapp-business) did NOT surface the Commerce Policy linkage in the fetched body - the governing linkage lives in the separate WhatsApp Business Policy / Commerce Policy documents, not the core ToS, so anyone checking only the ToS could wrongly conclude the link is absent. The linkage is nonetheless clearly present in the WhatsApp Business Policy.

Uncertainty/conflict notes: Meta restructures and re-headers these policies periodically; section heading labels (e.g., the small-model reported "Human Exploitation and Sexual Services" / "Adult Content and Sexually Positioned Products") should be treated as approximate, but the prohibition sentences themselves matched on direct fetch. No source conflicted with the claim. The claim is substantively correct; the only caveat is that "sexually suggestive adult products" is a paraphrase rather than a verbatim policy string, while the trafficking/prostitution/escort fragment IS verbatim.
verdicts.corrected: Substantively confirmed, with one wording nuance. The Meta Commerce Policy does contain the exact verbatim text "Commerce content may not promote any form of human trafficking, prostitution, escort or sexual services," and it does govern WhatsApp commerce/catalog use (the WhatsApp Business Policy states the Commerce Policy governs selling via WhatsApp Business Services including catalog and payments, and requires Catalog users to comply with the Meta Commerce Policy). However, the adult-products restriction is not a single verbatim phrase reading "sexually suggestive adult products"; the policy expresses it as two clauses: "Commerce content may not promote the buying, selling, or trading of adult products and may not position products in a sexually suggestive manner." So the trafficking/prostitution/escort prohibition is verbatim, while the adult-products portion is an accurate paraphrase rather than a verbatim quote.

verdicts.claim: The WhatsApp Business Messaging Policy separately prohibits 'sexually explicit materials or nudity' and classes adult products/services and dating as prohibited or specially-regulated verticals, meaning the prohibition does not depend solely on whether the catalog/commerce feature is used.
verdicts.explanation:
All three components of the claim are supported by the primary source (the official WhatsApp Business Messaging Policy at business.whatsapp.com/policy, which currently redirects to whatsappbusiness.com/policy/), cross-verified against Meta developer docs and third-party reproductions.

(1) SEPARATE SEXUAL-CONTENT PROHIBITION — CONFIRMED. The policy contains a standalone, general messaging-content provision: "Messages must not contain offensive content, for example sexually explicit materials or nudity, determined at our sole discretion." This sits in the Restrictions on Use section and is NOT scoped to commerce/catalog features; it applies to business messaging generally.

(2) ADULT PRODUCTS/SERVICES AND DATING AS PROHIBITED VERTICALS — CONFIRMED (and in fact stronger than the claim's hedged wording). Both "Adult products and services" and "Dating services" appear BY NAME in the policy's absolute-prohibition enumeration: "We prohibit use of the WhatsApp Business Services for buying, selling, promoting, or otherwise facilitating the exchange of certain regulated or restricted goods and services listed below" (alongside firearms, alcohol/tobacco, drugs, real-money gambling, etc.). They are NOT among the narrow Section 5 "Regulated Verticals" that get limited country-specific exceptions — that exception list covers only Online Gambling/Gaming, Over-the-Counter Drugs, and Alcohol. So these two categories are flatly prohibited, which fully satisfies (and exceeds) the claim's "prohibited or specially-regulated" framing.

(3) NOT DEPENDENT SOLELY ON CATALOG/COMMERCE USE — CONFIRMED. Multiple independent provisions operate in parallel: (a) the offensive-content prohibition above applies to messages generally; (b) the prohibition on adult products/services and dating covers "buying, selling, promoting, or otherwise facilitating the exchange" — the verbs "promoting" and "facilitating" reach beyond catalog transactions to ordinary promotional messaging; and (c) a SEPARATE, catalog-conditional bullet ("If you use Catalogs, or provide any other commerce experiences to sell or otherwise facilitate the exchange of goods or services prohibited by the Meta Commerce Policy, then we may prohibit you from using some or all of the WhatsApp Business Services"). Because (a) and (b) exist independently of the catalog-conditional clause (c), the prohibition does not hinge solely on whether the catalog/commerce feature is used.

CONFIDENCE / CAVEATS: High confidence on substance. The exact verbatim wording was confirmed via WebFetch of the live policy page plus a third-party reproduction (Messente); one WebSearch summary expressed doubt about the named verticals, but that was a model summary of snippets, contradicted by direct fetches of the actual page text, so I weight the page content as authoritative. Minor wording note on the claim: the categories are specifically "prohibited" (not merely "specially-regulated"), so the claim's disjunctive hedge slightly understates the severity but is not incorrect. The canonical policy URL (business.whatsapp.com/policy) 301-redirects to whatsappbusiness.com/policy/; both resolve to Meta's official policy content. Policy page indicated a last-updated context around March 2026, consistent with the 2026-06-10 evaluation date.

verdicts.claim: For the OFFICIAL WhatsApp Business Platform, Meta DOES correlate and enforce at the portfolio level (since Oct 2025): numbers grouped in one Business Manager share quality/limits, so one bad number can throttle or jeopardize the whole portfolio — this is documented, not speculation.
verdicts.explanation:
The core of the claim is CONFIRMED and documented, but the specific mechanism ("throttle") is imprecise and partly contradicted by the same Oct 2025 change.

CONFIRMED (primary/official): Effective October 7, 2025, Meta moved WhatsApp Business Platform messaging limits from per-phone-number to the Business Portfolio level. Meta's own developer documentation states messaging limits "are calculated and set at the business portfolio level and are shared by all business phone numbers within a portfolio" (developers.facebook.com/documentation/business-messaging/whatsapp/messaging-limits). All numbers in one portfolio (one Business Manager/Meta Business Portfolio) draw from a single shared limit pool; existing portfolios inherit the highest limit among their numbers, and new numbers immediately get the full portfolio limit (no warm-up). So "numbers grouped in one Business Manager share quality/limits" and "jeopardize the whole portfolio" is correct: tier upgrades for the whole portfolio depend on aggregate quality, and low quality/spam from ANY single number can block the entire portfolio from scaling up, and severe policy/spam violations are enforced at the WhatsApp Business Account level (blocks on sending and on adding numbers). This is documented, not speculation.

WHERE THE CLAIM IS IMPRECISE ("throttle"): The same Oct 7, 2025 overhaul explicitly REMOVED the old "Flagged" quality status that used to DOWNGRADE limits. As a result, a quality-rating drop no longer reduces/throttles your existing messaging limit. Multiple BSP sources summarizing Meta's change state plainly: "your messaging limit won't decrease if your message quality drops. Your quality rating only affects your ability to move to a higher tier — it doesn't cause downgrades." So a single "bad number" can JEOPARDIZE the portfolio (block scaling, and via policy enforcement trigger account-level blocks/bans), but it does NOT automatically "throttle" (lower) the portfolio's existing limit purely from a quality-rating drop. Real-time per-number quality/throughput throttling (e.g., pacing/blocked sends) and frequency-capping still exist, but those are not the portfolio-level limit-reduction the word "throttle" implies.

NET: Portfolio-level correlation and enforcement since Oct 2025 = TRUE and documented. "Share quality/limits" and "jeopardize the whole portfolio" = TRUE. "Throttle the whole portfolio" (in the sense of one bad number lowering the shared limit) = misleading; quality drops can block upward scaling and policy violations can hard-block/ban at account level, but quality-driven limit DOWNGRADES were removed in the same change.

CONFIDENCE/CAVEATS: High confidence on portfolio-level limits and the Flagged-removal (consistent across Meta docs + many independent BSP write-ups: Twilio, Wati, 8x8, Bird, Gallabox, Chatarmin, Convrs, DoubleTick). Lower confidence on the exact phrasing of policy-enforcement scope because Meta's developer docs (messaging-limits, policy-enforcement, upcoming-changes pages) render as JS navigation shells to automated fetchers and could not be quoted verbatim line-by-line; the substantive Meta text was recovered via search snippets and corroborated by BSP partners. No sources directly conflicted; the only tension is the loose word "throttle" vs. the documented removal of quality-based downgrades.
verdicts.corrected: Since October 7, 2025, the official WhatsApp Business Platform applies messaging limits and tier scaling at the Business Portfolio level (one Meta Business Portfolio / Business Manager): all phone numbers in a portfolio share a single messaging-limit pool, a portfolio inherits the highest limit among its numbers, and aggregate quality determines whether the whole portfolio can scale up. So one low-quality or policy-violating number CAN jeopardize the entire portfolio — it can block the portfolio from increasing its limit and, for spam/policy violations, trigger account-level enforcement (sending blocks, blocks on adding numbers, or bans). This is documented in Meta's developer docs, not speculation. Important nuance: the same Oct 2025 change REMOVED the old "Flagged" status, so a number's quality-rating DROP no longer reduces ("throttles") your existing portfolio limit — quality now only gates upward scaling, not downgrades. Real-time send pacing and per-user frequency caps still apply separately.
